AGGREGATION-S00002.description_expression: This signal identifies when a large variety of different AWS CloudTrail event ids are observed in a short period of time.  The event id's are generally related to reconnaissance.  This signal can indicate that an aggressive scan of AWS resources is underway.
AGGREGATION-S00002.match_expression:
metadata_vendor = 'Amazon AWS'
AND metadata_product = 'CloudTrail'
AND metadata_deviceEventId IN (
    'AwsApiCall-GetAccessKeyLastUsed',
    'AwsApiCall-GetAccountAuthorizationDetails',
    'AwsApiCall-GetAccountPasswordPolicy',
    'AwsApiCall-GetGroupPolicy',
    'AwsApiCall-GetUserPolicy',
    'AwsApiCall-ListAccessKeys',
    'AwsApiCall-ListGroupsForUser',
    'AwsApiCall-ListPolicies',
    'AwsApiCall-DescribeDBSnapshots',
    'AwsApiCall-GetAccountSummary',
    'AwsApiCall-GetCredentialReport',
    'AwsApiCall-GetGroup',
    'AwsApiCall-GetUser',
    'AwsApiCall-ListEntitiesForPolicy',
    'AwsApiCall-ListGroups',
    'AwsApiCall-ListUserPolicies',
    'AwsApiCall-ListUsers'
)
and NOT array_contains(listMatches, 'AWS_admin_ips')
and NOT array_contains(listMatches, 'AWS_admin_users')
AGGREGATION-S00002.name: AWS CloudTrail - Aggressive Reconnaissance
AGGREGATION-S00002.normalized_description: This signal identifies when a large variety of different AWS CloudTrail event ids are observed in a short period of time.  The event id's are generally related to reconnaissance.  This signal can indicate that an aggressive scan of AWS resources is underway.
AGGREGATION-S00002.normalized_summary: AWS CloudTrail reconnaissance activity detected by user: {{user_username}} from source IP: {{srcDevice_ip}}
AGGREGATION-S00002.summary_expression: AWS CloudTrail reconnaissance activity detected by user: {{user_username}} from source IP: {{srcDevice_ip}}

AGGREGATION-S00003.description_expression: Threat actors will attempt to enumerate various system settings in order to explore privilege escalation avenues or to locate potentially sensitive data.
AGGREGATION-S00003.match_expression:
metadata_vendor = 'Amazon AWS'
AND metadata_product = 'CloudTrail'
AND user_username_role != "cloudhealthreadonly"
AND fields["userIdentity.type"] != "AWSService"
AND user_username != "sumologic"
AND fields["requestParameters.roleSessionName"] != "sumologic"
AND !lower(fields["userIdentity.sessionContext.sessionIssuer.arn"]) matches /(sumoawsoquicksetup\-)/
AND !lower(fields["requestParameters.roleArn"]) matches /(cloudhealthreadonly)/
AND !lower(fields["requestParameters.roleArn"]) matches /(sumoawsoquicksetup\-)/
AND user_username_role != "awsservicerolefortrustedadvisor"
AND application = "lambda.amazonaws.com"
AGGREGATION-S00003.name: Suspicious AWS Lambda Enumeration
AGGREGATION-S00003.normalized_description: Threat actors will attempt to enumerate various system settings in order to explore privilege escalation avenues or to locate potentially sensitive data.
AGGREGATION-S00003.normalized_summary: {{user_username}} has recorded a number of AWS Lambda enumeration commands issued in rapid succession, indicating a potentially malicious system enumeration attempt.
AGGREGATION-S00003.summary_expression: {{user_username}} has recorded a number of AWS Lambda enumeration commands issued in rapid succession, indicating a potentially malicious system enumeration attempt.

AGGREGATION-S00005.commit_message: TLAB-447 Rule Status Modification from Prototype to Production.
AGGREGATION-S00005.description_expression: Threat actors will attempt to enuerate various system settings in order to explore privilige escalation avenues or to locate potentially sensitive data.
AGGREGATION-S00005.match_expression:
(
lower(commandLine) LIKE '%tasklist%'
 or lower(commandLine) LIKE '%systeminfo%'
 or lower(commandLine) LIKE '%nbtstat%'
 or lower(commandLine) LIKE '%qprocess%'
 or lower(commandLine) LIKE '%nslookup%'
 or lower(commandLine) LIKE '%hostname.exe%'
 or lower(commandLine) LIKE '%netstat%'
 or lower(commandLine) LIKE '%whoami%'
 or lower(commandLine) LIKE '%ipconfig%'
 or lower(commandLine) LIKE '%arp -a%'
 or lower(commandLine) LIKE '%quser%'
 and not array_contains(listMatches,'vuln_scanners')
 and device_hostname is not null
AND NOT lower(parentBaseImage) LIKE '%\qualys\qualysagent\qualysagent.exe') AND (NOT (file_path LIKE '%Microsoft\Windows Defender Advanced Threat Protection\DataCollection\%'))
AGGREGATION-S00005.name: Suspicious System Enumeration Occurring in Quick Succession
AGGREGATION-S00005.name_expression: Suspicious System Enumeration Occurring in Quick Succession
AGGREGATION-S00005.normalized_description: Threat actors will attempt to enuerate various system settings in order to explore privilige escalation avenues or to locate potentially sensitive data.
AGGREGATION-S00005.normalized_signal_name: Suspicious System Enumeration Occurring in Quick Succession
AGGREGATION-S00005.normalized_summary: {{device_hostname}} has recorded a number of system enumeration commands issued in rapid sucession, indicating a potentially malicious system enumeration attempt.
AGGREGATION-S00005.summary_expression: {{device_hostname}} has recorded a number of system enumeration commands issued in rapid sucession, indicating a potentially malicious system enumeration attempt.

AGGREGATION-S00006.commit_message: Campaign 5 Rule Content and Test Case Creation
AGGREGATION-S00006.description_expression: Threat actors will aim to enumerate various permissions and settings on hosts with Docker installed, this enumeration can potentially lead to explotiation avenues.
AGGREGATION-S00006.match_expression:
metadata_deviceEventId !matches /(System Call\-257|System Call\-41)/
AND
( lower(commandLine) matches /(curl \-s \-\-unix\-socket \/var\/run\/docker.sock)/
or lower(commandLine) matches /(docker info)/
or lower(commandLine) matches /(docker ps)/
or lower(commandLine) matches /(grep(.*).\/(docker|kubepod|lxc).*\/proc\/1\/cgroup)/ )
AGGREGATION-S00006.name: Docker Enumeration Detected on Host
AGGREGATION-S00006.normalized_description: Threat actors will aim to enumerate various permissions and settings on hosts with Docker installed, this enumeration can potentially lead to explotiation avenues.
AGGREGATION-S00006.normalized_summary: A number of Docker enumeration commands were issued on {{device_hostname}}
AGGREGATION-S00006.summary_expression: A number of Docker enumeration commands were issued on {{device_hostname}}

AGGREGATION-S00007.description_expression: This rule detects when a user has utilized multiple distinct operating systems when performing authentication through Okta. This activity could potentially indicate credential theft or a general session anomaly. Examine other Okta related events surrounding the time period for this signal, pivoting off the username value to examine if any other suspicious activity has taken place. If this rule is generating false positives, adjust the threshold value and consider excluding certain user accounts via tuning expression or a match list.

If this rule is generating false positives, adjust the threshold value and consider excluding certain user accounts via tuning expression or a match list.
AGGREGATION-S00007.normalized_summary: {{user_username}} has utilized a number of distinct operating systems which has crossed the threshold (>2) value within a 30-minute time period to perform Okta authentication.
AGGREGATION-S00007.summary_expression: {{user_username}} has utilized a number of distinct operating systems which has crossed the threshold (>2) value within a 30-minute time period to perform Okta authentication.

CHAIN-S00002.description: CVE-2020-1472 can be exploited by attackers to hijack enterprise servers due to Netlogon cryptographic weaknesses. The vulnerability allows an attacker to set a password for the computer account of an Active Directory Domain Controller, which can then be abused to pull credentials from the Domain Controller. This rule detects the domain controller computer account being changed after a successful anonymous login occurred.

expressions_and_limits.expression:
metadata_vendor = 'Microsoft'
AND metadata_product = 'Windows'
AND metadata_deviceEventId = 'Security-4624'
AND lower(user_username) = 'anonymous logon'

metadata_vendor = 'Microsoft'
AND metadata_product = 'Windows'
AND metadata_deviceEventId = 'Security-4742'
AND lower(user_username) = 'anonymous logon'

CHAIN-S00002.name: Windows - Suspicious Anonymous Logon Activity - Zerologon Behavior(CVE-2020-1472)
CHAIN-S00002.normalized_description: CVE-2020-1472 can be exploited by attackers to hijack enterprise servers due to Netlogon cryptographic weaknesses. The vulnerability allows an attacker to set a password for the computer account of an Active Directory Domain Controller, which can then be abused to pull credentials from the Domain Controller. This rule detects the domain controller computer account being changed after a successful anonymous login occurred.
CHAIN-S00002.normalized_signal_name: Windows - Suspicious Anonymous Logon Activity - Zerologon Behavior(CVE-2020-1472)
CHAIN-S00002.normalized_summary: Detected anonymous logon activity on host: {{device_hostname}}
CHAIN-S00002.summary_expression: Detected anonymous logon activity on host: {{device_hostname}}

CHAIN-S00004.description: Detects pivoting to an internal host from another internal host. Attackers will connect to the ADMIN$ share of an internal host and upload a program to execute remote commands to fully compromise the host.

expressions_and_limits.expression:
metadata_vendor = 'Microsoft'
AND metadata_product = 'Windows'
AND metadata_deviceEventId = 'Security-5140'
AND (
    resource = 'ADMIN$'
    OR
    resource LIKE '%\ADMIN$'
    )

metadata_vendor = 'Microsoft'
AND metadata_product = 'Windows'
AND metadata_deviceEventId = 'Security-4697'
AND NOT array_contains(listMatches, 'verified_applications')
AND NOT array_contains(listMatches, 'admin_username')

CHAIN-S00004.name: Lateral Movement Using the Windows Hidden Admin Share
CHAIN-S00004.normalized_description: Detects pivoting to an internal host from another internal host. Attackers will connect to the ADMIN$ share of an internal host and upload a program to execute remote commands to fully compromise the host.
CHAIN-S00004.normalized_signal_name: Lateral Movement Using the Windows Hidden Admin Share

CHAIN-S00007.description: Attackers will sometimes create and subsequently delete user accounts to perform post-exploitation activity in an attempted counter-forensic measure.

expressions_and_limits.expression: metadata_deviceEventId = 'useradd-new-user'
metadata_deviceEventId = 'userdel'

CHAIN-S00007.name: User Created and Quickly Deleted on Linux Machine
CHAIN-S00007.normalized_description: Attackers will sometimes create and subsequently delete user accounts to perform post-exploitation activity in an attempted counter-forensic measure.
CHAIN-S00007.normalized_signal_name: User Created and Quickly Deleted on Linux Machine
CHAIN-S00007.normalized_summary: User {{user_username}} was created and deleted within 1 hour on host {{device_hostname}}
CHAIN-S00007.summary_expression: User {{user_username}} was created and deleted within 1 hour on host {{device_hostname}}

CHAIN-S00008.description: Detects a series of failed logins followed by a successful login. This could indicate that an attacker was successful in guessing a user's password and has compromised their account. This rule does not leverage authentication logs with a normalizedAction of domainLogon and does not have a Domain version, meaning that Windows workstation logging is required to achieve full visibility.

expressions_and_limits.expression:
objectType = 'Authentication'
AND normalizedAction = 'logon'
AND success = false
AND NOT (
metadata_deviceEventId = 'Security-4776' AND (array_contains(listMatches, 'domain_controllers'))
)
AND NOT
    (metadata_vendor IN ('Microsoft','Intersect Alliance')
    AND metadata_product IN ('Windows','Snare Enterprise Agent for Windows')
    AND user_username like '%$%')
AND NOT
    (metadata_vendor = 'Microsoft'
    AND metadata_product = 'Azure'
    AND fields['resultType'] = '700082')
AND NOT array_contains(listMatches, 'vuln_scanners')

objectType = 'Authentication'
AND normalizedAction = 'logon'
AND success = true
AND NOT array_contains(listMatches, 'vuln_scanners')

CHAIN-S00008.name: Successful Brute Force
CHAIN-S00008.normalized_description: Detects a series of failed logins followed by a successful login. This could indicate that an attacker was successful in guessing a user's password and has compromised their account. This rule does not leverage authentication logs with a normalizedAction of domainLogon and does not have a Domain version, meaning that Windows workstation logging is required to achieve full visibility.

CHAIN-S00010.description: This rule looks for the installation of an application by a non-Sytem user quickly followed by the execution of cmd.exe with System privileges on the same host. This indicates a privilege escalation attempt was likely successful and correlates directly to CVE-2021-41379. The rule is designed to work with standard Security and System event logging, but Sysmon process logging can substitute in place of event ID 4688.

expressions_and_limits.expression:
metadata_deviceEventId IN ('System-7045','Security-4697','7045','4697')
AND NOT user_userId = 'S-1-5-18'

metadata_deviceEventId IN ('Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon/Operational-1','4688','Security-4688')
AND baseImage LIKE '%\cmd.exe'
AND (lower(fields['EventData.IntegrityLevel']) = 'system' OR fields['EventData.MandatoryLabel'] = 'S-1-16-16384')
AND (lower(parentBaseImage) LIKE '%\elevation_service.exe' OR lower(parentCommandLine) LIKE '%\elevation_service.exe% /svc%')

CHAIN-S00010.name: Service Installation Followed By Elevated CMD Prompt
CHAIN-S00010.normalized_description: This rule looks for the installation of an application by a non-Sytem user quickly followed by the execution of cmd.exe with System privileges on the same host. This indicates a privilege escalation attempt was likely successful and correlates directly to CVE-2021-41379. The rule is designed to work with standard Security and System event logging, but Sysmon process logging can substitute in place of event ID 4688.
CHAIN-S00010.normalized_signal_name: Service Installation Followed By Elevated CMD Prompt
CHAIN-S00010.normalized_summary: A possible privilege escalation attempt via service installation was detected on host {{device_hostname}}
CHAIN-S00010.summary_expression: A possible privilege escalation attempt via service installation was detected on host {{device_hostname}}

AND metadata_deviceEventId = "Administrative"
AND description = "MICROSOFT.RESOURCES/DEPLOYMENTS/WRITE"
AND lower(resource) matches /(microsoft\.automationaccount)/ 
AND success = true

metadata_vendor = 'Microsoft'
AND metadata_product = "Azure"
AND metadata_deviceEventId = "Administrative"
AND description = "MICROSOFT.AUTOMATION/AUTOMATIONACCOUNTS/WEBHOOKS/WRITE"
AND success = true

CHAIN-S00014.commit_message: Campaign 5 Rule Content and Test Case Creation
CHAIN-S00014.description: A Docker container running with the privileged flag may be exploited by threat actors, potentially resulting in an escape from the Docker container to the host that it is running on. This can result in various privilege escalation opportunities.

expressions_and_limits.expression: lower(commandLine) matches /(sh.\-c.echo 0 \>(.*?)cgroup\.procs)/
lower(commandLine) matches /(mkdir.\-p.\/sys\/.*cgroup)/

CHAIN-S00014.name: Potential Docker container escape via Cgroups
CHAIN-S00014.normalized_description: A Docker container running with the privileged flag may be exploited by threat actors, potentially resulting in an escape from the Docker container to the host that it is running on. This can result in various privilege escalation opportunities.

CHAIN-S00014.normalized_summary: Potential Docker container escape detected on {{device_hostname}}
CHAIN-S00014.summary_expression: Potential Docker container escape detected on {{device_hostname}}

CHAIN-S00015.commit_message: Campaign 5 Rule Content and Test Case Creation
CHAIN-S00015.description: This alert looks for a number of search expressions that result in a suspicious Linux execution chain. Specifically, a file that is created in a users' home directory or in /tmp, followed by a chmod and file execution, as well as the process making a network connection.

expressions_and_limits.expression:
action = "FileCreate"
and metadata_vendor = "Linux"
and changeTarget matches /(\/home|^\/tmp)/
and changeTarget !matches /(clearsigned.message)/

metadata_vendor = "Linux"
and commandLine matches /(chmod)/
and fields["CurrentDirectory"] matches /(\/home|^\/tmp)/

metadata_vendor = "Linux"
and objectType = "EndpointProcess"
and baseImage matches /(\/home|^\/tmp)/

metadata_vendor = "Linux"
and objectType = "Network"
and baseImage matches /(\/home|^\/tmp)/

CHAIN-S00015.name: Suspicious Linux Execution Chain
CHAIN-S00015.normalized_description: This alert looks for a number of search expressions that result in a suspicious Linux execution chain. Specifically, a file that is created in a users' home directory or in /tmp, followed by a chmod and file execution, as well as the process making a network connection.
CHAIN-S00015.normalized_summary: A suspicious execution chain has ran by {{user_username}}
CHAIN-S00015.summary_expression: A suspicious execution chain has ran by {{user_username}}

CHAIN-S00016.commit_message: TLAB-746 macOS - Suspicious Osascript Execution and Network Activity
CHAIN-S00016.description: This rule utilizes Jamf telemetry and looks for osascript execution with a suspicious parent process indicating execution from a shell or terminal in addition to the osascript process making network connections to an external IP address.

expressions_and_limits.expression:
baseImage = "/usr/bin/osascript"
and !isNull(commandLine)
and parentBaseImage matches /(\/bin)/

metadata_vendor = "Jamf"
and metadata_product = "Jamf"
and metadata_deviceEventId = "AUE_CONNECT"
and parentBaseImage matches /(\/bin)/
and baseImage = "/usr/bin/osascript"
and !isNull(dstDevice_ip)
and dstDevice_ip_isInternal = false

CHAIN-S00018.description: This signal looks for a USB drive being mounted on a Windows host followed by a file creation event with the file name of "autorun.inf" within a 5-minute time frame. This activity could be indicative of an attempt at lateral movement or initial access avenues through a USB device. Ensure that the machine in question is authorized to use USB devices and look for other file creation events from this host around the same time frame. This rule logic utilizes Sysmon file creation events, which need to be enabled and configured on relevant assets.

expressions_and_limits.expression:
metadata_vendor = "Microsoft"
and metadata_deviceEventId = "Security-6416"
and fields["EventData.ClassName"] = "WPD"

action = "FileCreate"
and lower(file_path) matches /autorun\.inf/

CHAIN-S00018.name: Autorun file created after USB disk mount on host
CHAIN-S00018.normalized_description: This signal looks for a USB drive being mounted on a Windows host followed by a file creation event with the file name of "autorun.inf" within a 5-minute time frame. This activity could be indicative of an attempt at lateral movement or initial access avenues through a USB device. Ensure that the machine in question is authorized to use USB devices and look for other file creation events from this host around the same time frame. This rule logic utilizes Sysmon file creation events, which need to be enabled and configured on relevant assets.
CHAIN-S00018.normalized_signal_name: Autorun file created after USB disk mount on host
CHAIN-S00018.normalized_summary: An autorun.inf was created on a USB device shortly after mounting the drive on  {{device_hostname}}
CHAIN-S00018.summary_expression: An autorun.inf was created on a USB device shortly after mounting the drive on  {{device_hostname}}

CHAIN-S00019.description: This alert looks for two events in a particular order, the first event involves a certificate template being loaded with a certificate request agent application policy. The second event involves the same event type, but with a client authentication application policy. Together, these events may indicate exploitation of an “enrollment agent” misconfiguration. Threat actors may abuse this misconfiguration to forge certificates and Kerberos Ticket Granting Tickets (TGT). Specifically, this misconfiguration can exploit the "ESC3" attack path via https://posts.specterops.io/certified-pre-owned-d95910965cd2. If this event is deemed suspicious, look for authentication events (4624) that utilize this certificate template. Also ensure that this change was expected and approved within the environment.

expressions_and_limits.expression:
metadata_vendor = "Microsoft" 
and metadata_deviceEventId = "Security-4898"
and lower(fields["EventData.TemplateContent"]) matches /(mspki\-certificate\-application.policy.*\n.*1\.3\.6\.1\.4\.1\.311\.20\.2\.1.certificate request agent)/

metadata_vendor = "Microsoft" 
and metadata_deviceEventId = "Security-4898"
and lower(fields["EventData.TemplateContent"]) matches /(mspki\-certificate\-application.policy.*\n.*1\.3\.6\.1\.5\.5\.7\.3\.2.client authentication)/

CHAIN-S00019.name: Potential Active Directory Certificate Services Enrollment Agent Misconfiguration
CHAIN-S00019.normalized_description: This alert looks for two events in a particular order, the first event involves a certificate template being loaded with a certificate request agent application policy. The second event involves the same event type, but with a client authentication application policy. Together, these events may indicate exploitation of an “enrollment agent” misconfiguration. Threat actors may abuse this misconfiguration to forge certificates and Kerberos Ticket Granting Tickets (TGT). Specifically, this misconfiguration can exploit the "ESC3" attack path via https://posts.specterops.io/certified-pre-owned-d95910965cd2. If this event is deemed suspicious, look for authentication events (4624) that utilize this certificate template. Also ensure that this change was expected and approved within the environment.

CHAIN-S00019.normalized_summary: Potential Active Directory Certificate Services Enrollment Agent Misconfiguration on {{device_hostname}}
CHAIN-S00019.summary_expression: Potential Active Directory Certificate Services Enrollment Agent Misconfiguration on {{device_hostname}}

CHAIN-S00020.description: This signal looks for a single user explicitly denying at least two (2) multi factor authentication prompts, followed by a successful Okta login via multi factor authentication within a twenty-five (25) minute window. This logic is designed to catch successful MFA fatigue type attacks. If you find this signal is triggering on legitimate events, consider excluding certain users via tuning expressions. You can also consider tweaking the time window within the "Grouped by" portion of the rule.

expressions_and_limits.expression:
metadata_vendor = "Okta"
and metadata_deviceEventId = "user.authentication.auth_via_mfa"
and fields["debugContext.debugData.pushOnlyResponseType"] = "OV_RESPONSE_DENY"

metadata_vendor = "Okta"
and metadata_deviceEventId = "user.authentication.auth_via_mfa"
and success = true

CHAIN-S00021.description:
Context:
An attacker may use a pipeline for malicious activity such as introducing malicious code into production or executing malicious code in an agent pool. The attacker likely will delete the pipeline after achieving their objectives in order to cover their tracks.

Detection:
This detection monitors for the creation and deletion of the same pipeline within a short period (by default, a day).

Recommended Actions:
Investigate whether the rapid creation and deletion of the pipeline was an expected activity. 
Review build artifacts resulting from the pipeline for malicious code. 
Investigate the user for indications of account compromise or insider threat activity.

Tuning Recommendations:
Adjust the time constraints of the rule from one day to one which aligns better to the practices in your organization. 
If there are known use cases for rapid creation and deletion of pipelines in your organization, add a tuning expression to exclude the distinguishing metadata such as the username.

Credits:
This detection is based on the Azure Sentinel AzDOPipelineCreatedDeletedOneDay.yaml rule.

expressions_and_limits.expression:
metadata_vendor = "Microsoft"
AND metadata_product = "Azure DevOps Auditing"
AND metadata_deviceEventId = "AzureDevOpsAuditEvent"
AND (
    action = "Release.ReleasePipelineCreated"
    OR
    action = "Pipelines.PipelineCreated"
)

metadata_vendor = "Microsoft"
AND metadata_product = "Azure DevOps Auditing"
AND metadata_deviceEventId = "AzureDevOpsAuditEvent"
AND (
    action LIKE "%ReleasePipelineDeleted"
    OR
    action = "Pipelines.PipelineDeleted"
)

CHAIN-S00021.name: Azure DevOps - Pipeline Created and Deleted within a Short Period
CHAIN-S00021.normalized_description:
Context:
An attacker may use a pipeline for malicious activity such as introducing malicious code into production or executing malicious code in an agent pool. The attacker likely will delete the pipeline after achieving their objectives in order to cover their tracks.

Credits:
This detection is based on the Azure Sentinel AzDOPipelineCreatedDeletedOneDay.yaml rule.
CHAIN-S00021.normalized_signal_name: Azure DevOps - Pipeline Created and Deleted within a Short Period
CHAIN-S00021.normalized_summary: User: {{user_username}} has been observed creating and deleting Azure DevOps Pipelines in a short period of time
CHAIN-S00021.summary_expression: User: {{user_username}} has been observed creating and deleting Azure DevOps Pipelines in a short period of time

CHAIN-S00022.description:
Context:
An attacker may create Azure DevOps Agent Pools for malicious activity that are separate from an organization’s pools.

Detection:
This detection monitors for the creation and deletion of Agent Pools within 5 days by the same user, with the intent of finding Agent Pools active for short durations.

Recommended Actions:
If an alert occurs, investigate the actions taken by the account to determine if this is normal operation of deleting pools or if this suspicious activity. 

Tuning Recommendations: 
If necessary, add a tuning expression for users who frequently create and delete agent pools within the time period specified.
If necessary, adjust the "within" time period to better represent what is considered a short time period in your organization. Note that 5 days is the maximum time period you can specify for a chain rules.

Credits: 
This rule is loosely based on the Azure Sentinel ADOAgentPoolCreatedDeleted.yaml detection.

expressions_and_limits.expression:
metadata_vendor = "Microsoft"
AND metadata_product = "Azure DevOps Auditing"
AND metadata_deviceEventId = "AzureDevOpsAuditEvent"
AND action = "Library.AgentPoolCreated"

metadata_vendor = "Microsoft"
AND metadata_product = "Azure DevOps Auditing"
AND metadata_deviceEventId = "AzureDevOpsAuditEvent"
AND action = "Library.AgentPoolDeleted"

Recommended Actions:
If an alert occurs, investigate the actions taken by the account to determine if this is normal operation of deleting pools or if this suspicious activity. 

Tuning Recommendations: 
If necessary, add a tuning expression for users who frequently create and delete agent pools within the time period specified.
If necessary, adjust the "within" time period to better represent what is considered a short time period in your organization. Note that 5 days is the maximum time period you can specify for a chain rules.

Credits: 
This rule is loosely based on the Azure Sentinel ADOAgentPoolCreatedDeleted.yaml detection.

CHAIN-S00023.commit_message: TLAB-2265: Updates to Brute Force RDP rules (#5865)
CHAIN-S00023.description:
Detection:
This rule correlates a high number of failed authentication attempts with  a successful remote interactive login (such as via RDP) coming from the same source IP address and user account. 

Context:
An adversary may discover an account password by using brute force tools against hosts that allow Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) connections. Upon discovering the correct password the attacker proceeds to log in via RDP.

Recommended Actions:
Determine whether this activity was benign, ideally by contacting the user. If the activity is determined to be malicious, log out all sessions from the compromised account and force a password reset. Investigate the activities of the user account originating from the RDP host to look for signs of persistence and lateral movement. Consider taking the RDP host offline for investigation. Block all connections from the source host.

Tuning Recommendations: 
Add the IP addresses of your vulnerability scanners to the "vuln_scanners" list. Adjust the record counts that trigger this rule if necessary.

expressions_and_limits.expression:
logonType = 'Network'
AND normalizedAction = 'domainLogon'
AND normalizedCause IN ('incorrect password', 'incorrect username')
AND success = false

logonType = 'RemoteInteractive'
AND normalizedAction = 'logon'
AND success = true
AND objectClassification = "Authentication"

CHAIN-S00023.name: Administrative Remote Interactive Brute Force Login
CHAIN-S00023.normalized_description:
Detection:
This rule correlates a high number of failed authentication attempts with  a successful remote interactive login (such as via RDP) coming from the same source IP address and user account. 

Context:
An adversary may discover an account password by using brute force tools against hosts that allow Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) connections. Upon discovering the correct password the attacker proceeds to log in via RDP.

Recommended Actions:
Determine whether this activity was benign, ideally by contacting the user. If the activity is determined to be malicious, log out all sessions from the compromised account and force a password reset. Investigate the activities of the user account originating from the RDP host to look for signs of persistence and lateral movement. Consider taking the RDP host offline for investigation. Block all connections from the source host.

Tuning Recommendations: 
Add the IP addresses of your vulnerability scanners to the "vuln_scanners" list. Adjust the record counts that trigger this rule if necessary.
CHAIN-S00023.normalized_signal_name: Administrative Remote Interactive Brute Force Login
CHAIN-S00023.normalized_summary: Successful administrative remote interactive brute force login from IP: {{srcDevice_ip}} to Host:  {{dstDevice_hostname}}  from user {{user_username}}
CHAIN-S00023.summary_expression: Successful administrative remote interactive brute force login from IP: {{srcDevice_ip}} to Host:  {{dstDevice_hostname}}  from user {{user_username}}

CHAIN-S00024.commit_message: TLAB-2265: Updates to Brute Force RDP rules (#5865)
CHAIN-S00024.description:
Detection:
This rule correlates a high number of failed authentication attempts with repeated inbound connections over port 3389 (the default RDP port).

Scenario:
An adversary may attempt to discover an account password by using brute force tools against hosts that allow Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) connections.

Recommended Actions:
Review the destination host and consider whether blocking RDP connections to it from the Internet is appropriate. Review the source IP addresses of the brute force activity and consider blocking it at the firewall. Review the account names that are being used in the brute force attacks and consider forcing password resets for the accounts.

Tuning Recommendations: 
Add the IP addresses of your vulnerability scanners to the "vuln_scanners" list. 
Note that this rule is more likely to trigger when the destination host is a multi-user Terminal Server or if more than one user connects to the host from the same IP address (such as via NAT). Increase the record count that trigger this rule if necessary.

expressions_and_limits.expression:
logonType = 'Network'
AND normalizedAction = 'domainLogon'
AND normalizedCause IN ('incorrect password', 'incorrect username')
AND success = false

dstPort = '3389'
AND dstDevice_ip_isInternal = true
and not array_contains(listMatches,'vuln_scanners')

CHAIN-S00024.name: RDP Brute Force Login Attempt
CHAIN-S00024.normalized_description:
Detection:
This rule correlates a high number of failed authentication attempts with repeated inbound connections over port 3389 (the default RDP port).

Scenario:
An adversary may attempt to discover an account password by using brute force tools against hosts that allow Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) connections.

Recommended Actions:
Review the destination host and consider whether blocking RDP connections to it from the Internet is appropriate. Review the source IP addresses of the brute force activity and consider blocking it at the firewall. Review the account names that are being used in the brute force attacks and consider forcing password resets for the accounts.

Tuning Recommendations: 
Add the IP addresses of your vulnerability scanners to the "vuln_scanners" list. 
Note that this rule is more likely to trigger when the destination host is a multi-user Terminal Server or if more than one user connects to the host from the same IP address (such as via NAT). Increase the record count that trigger this rule if necessary.
CHAIN-S00024.normalized_summary: Attempted brute force attack from IP: {{srcDevice_ip}} to Host:  {{dstDevice_hostname}}
CHAIN-S00024.summary_expression: Attempted brute force attack from IP: {{srcDevice_ip}} to Host:  {{dstDevice_hostname}}

FIRST-S00025.commit_message: INVS-1462: Update First Seen Rules to be contentType of ANOMALY (#5089)
FIRST-S00025.description_expression: Observes for SMB traffic allowed through the firewall.
FIRST-S00025.filter_expression:
(
  (srcDevice_ip_isInternal = false AND dstDevice_ip_isInternal = true)
OR
(srcDevice_ip_isInternal = true AND dstDevice_ip_isInternal = false)
)
AND (
    (   
        dstPort in (139,445)
    AND lower(ipProtocol) in ('6','tcp')
    )
OR
    (
       lower(tcpProtocol) = 'smb'
    OR lower(application) = 'smb'
    )
)    
AND
(
        objectType like 'Network%'
    AND  lower(action) rlike '(allow|accept)'
)
FIRST-S00025.name: First Seen SMB Allowed Traffic From IP
FIRST-S00025.name_expression: First Seen SMB Allowed Traffic From IP
FIRST-S00025.normalized_description: Observes for SMB traffic allowed through the firewall.
FIRST-S00025.normalized_signal_name: First Seen SMB Allowed Traffic From IP
FIRST-S00025.normalized_summary: First seen allowed SMB traffic from IP {{srcDevice_ip}} to {{dstDevice_ip}}
FIRST-S00025.summary_expression: First seen allowed SMB traffic from IP {{srcDevice_ip}} to {{dstDevice_ip}}

FIRST-S00026.description_expression: Attackers can attempt to hijack enterprise servers exploiting Netlogon cryptographic weaknesses. The vulnerability allows an attacker to set a password for the computer account of an Active Directory Domain Controller, which can then be abused to pull credentials from the Domain Controller. This rule detects fist seen anonymous user attempt a change on a domain controller computer account.
FIRST-S00026.filter_expression:
metadata_vendor = 'Microsoft'
AND metadata_product = 'Windows'
AND metadata_deviceEventId = 'Security-4742'
AND lower(user_username) = 'anonymous logon'
AND array_contains(listMatches, 'domain_controllers')
FIRST-S00026.name: First Seen Anonymous Logon Change Activity to Domain Controller
FIRST-S00026.name_expression: First Seen Anonymous Logon Change Activity to Domain Controller host
FIRST-S00026.normalized_description: Attackers can attempt to hijack enterprise servers exploiting Netlogon cryptographic weaknesses. The vulnerability allows an attacker to set a password for the computer account of an Active Directory Domain Controller, which can then be abused to pull credentials from the Domain Controller. This rule detects fist seen anonymous user attempt a change on a domain controller computer account.
FIRST-S00026.normalized_signal_name: First Seen Anonymous Logon Change Activity to Domain Controller host
FIRST-S00026.normalized_summary: Detected anonymous logon activity on domain controller host: {{device_hostname}}
FIRST-S00026.summary_expression: Detected anonymous logon activity on domain controller host: {{device_hostname}}

FIRST-S00028.commit_message: TLABS-1393 - Update summary of First Seen Common Windows Recon Commands From User
FIRST-S00028.description_expression: Detects a set of commands often used in recon stages by different attack groups.
FIRST-S00028.filter_expression:
(
lower(commandLine) LIKE '%tasklist%'
 or lower(commandLine) LIKE '%systeminfo%'
 or lower(commandLine) LIKE '%nbtstat%'
 or lower(commandLine) LIKE '%qprocess%'
 or lower(commandLine) LIKE '%nslookup%'
 or lower(commandLine) LIKE '%hostname.exe%'
 or lower(commandLine) LIKE '%netstat -an%')
 and not array_contains(listMatches,'vuln_scanners')
 and device_hostname is not null
AND NOT lower(parentBaseImage) LIKE '%\qualys\qualysagent\qualysagent.exe'
FIRST-S00028.name: First Seen Common Windows Recon Commands From User
FIRST-S00028.name_expression: First Seen Common Windows Recon Commands From User
FIRST-S00028.normalized_description: Detects a set of commands often used in recon stages by different attack groups.
FIRST-S00028.normalized_signal_name: First Seen Common Windows Recon Commands From User
FIRST-S00028.normalized_summary: Detected reconnaissance activity from user: {{user_username}} on host: {{device_hostname}}
FIRST-S00028.summary_expression: Detected reconnaissance activity from user: {{user_username}} on host: {{device_hostname}}

FIRST-S00030.description_expression: External connections over the internet to port 445 could be indictative of hash leak attempts, including exploitation attempts for vulnerabilities such as CVE-2023-2397. This alert looks at a source IP address making a connection to a new external destination IP address since the baseline period.
FIRST-S00030.filter_expression:
dstPort = "445"
AND isPublicIP(dstDevice_ip)
AND srcDevice_ip_isInternal = true
FIRST-S00030.name: First Seen Outbound Connection to External IP Address on Port 445 from IP Address
FIRST-S00030.name_expression: First Seen Outbound Connection to External IP Address on Port 445 from IP Address

FIRST-S00038.description_expression: First Seen execution of Wget by a user from a device. The Wget tool is designed to retrieve files using various internet protocols in a programmatic manner, it is often abused by threat actors to download various files as part of broader executions. If this usage of Wget comes from an unexpected user, it is recommended that the command line value be reviewed and the URL which was used as part of Wget command be investigated.

FIRST-S00038.normalized_summary: {{user_username}} has executed a Wget command from {{device_hostname}}
FIRST-S00038.summary_expression: {{user_username}} has executed a Wget command from {{device_hostname}}

FIRST-S00039.description_expression: A user has executed a mdfind command not seen since the baseline period. The mdfind binary can be used to find passwords and other sensitive info on the host  system. This rule utilizes data from the "LOOBins" project: https://github.com/infosecB/LOOBins - more information regarding this specific binary can be found via: https://www.loobins.io/binaries/mdfind/
FIRST-S00039.filter_expression:
baseImage = "/usr/bin/mdfind"
and lower(parentBaseImage) !matches /homebrew\/library\/homebrew/
and parentBaseImage != "/Applications/Docker.app/Contents/MacOS/com.docker.backend"
and commandLine != "mdfind,kMDItemCFBundleIdentifier == 'com.grammarly.ProjectLlama'"
and commandLine != "/usr/bin/mdfind,kMDItemCFBundleIdentifier == com.apple.dt.Xcode || kMDItemCFBundleIdentifier == com.apple.Xcode"
FIRST-S00039.name: First Seen mdfind Usage from User
FIRST-S00039.name_expression: First Seen mdfind Usage from User
FIRST-S00039.normalized_description: A user has executed a mdfind command not seen since the baseline period. The mdfind binary can be used to find passwords and other sensitive info on the host  system. This rule utilizes data from the "LOOBins" project: https://github.com/infosecB/LOOBins - more information regarding this specific binary can be found via: https://www.loobins.io/binaries/mdfind/
FIRST-S00039.normalized_signal_name: First Seen mdfind Usage from User
FIRST-S00039.normalized_summary: {{user_username}} has executed a mdfind command from {{device_hostname}}
FIRST-S00039.summary_expression: {{user_username}} has executed a mdfind command from {{device_hostname}}

FIRST-S00046.name: First Seen Client Generating MailItemsAccessed Event from User
FIRST-S00046.name_expression: First Seen Client Generating MailItemsAccessed Event from {{user_username}}
FIRST-S00046.normalized_description: This alert looks at a First Seen client accessing an Office 365/Exchange mail box item. The MailItemsAccessed may not always be enabled within an Entra/Azure/Office 365 tenant and is dependent on Microsoft licensing requirements. See the following guide from CISA for additional information on this event type and investigation steps: https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa23-193a
FIRST-S00046.normalized_signal_name: First Seen Client Generating MailItemsAccessed Event from {{user_username}}
FIRST-S00046.normalized_summary: A First Seen client was observed as accessing an Office 365 mail item since the baseline period.
FIRST-S00046.summary_expression: A First Seen client was observed as accessing an Office 365 mail item since the baseline period.

FIRST-S00047.description_expression: This rule will trigger when a new ASN value is associated with a successful Entra ID sign in event for a particular username since the baseline period. This may be suspicious activity as a users IP address may change periodically, but typically users authenticate from a set of ASNs (one ASN value for their home network, another ASN value for their mobile device ) – a sign in with a new ASN not seen since the baseline period could be indicative of credential theft. Look at other events occurring for the user in question for the same time period to ascertain whether access was malicious or benign.
FIRST-S00047.filter_expression:
metadata_product = "Azure"
and objectType = "Authentication"
and success = true
and metadata_deviceEventId = "SignInLogs"
and errorCode = 0
FIRST-S00047.name: First Seen ASN Associated with User for a Successful Azure AD Sign In Event
FIRST-S00047.name_expression: First Seen ASN Associated with User for a Successful Azure AD Sign In Event
FIRST-S00047.normalized_description: This rule will trigger when a new ASN value is associated with a successful Entra ID sign in event for a particular username since the baseline period. This may be suspicious activity as a users IP address may change periodically, but typically users authenticate from a set of ASNs (one ASN value for their home network, another ASN value for their mobile device ) – a sign in with a new ASN not seen since the baseline period could be indicative of credential theft. Look at other events occurring for the user in question for the same time period to ascertain whether access was malicious or benign.
FIRST-S00047.normalized_signal_name: First Seen ASN Associated with User for a Successful Azure AD Sign In Event
FIRST-S00047.normalized_summary: {{user_username}} has sucessfully signed into an Azure resource with a first seen ASN of {{device_ip_asnOrg}} since the baseline period.
FIRST-S00047.summary_expression: {{user_username}} has sucessfully signed into an Azure resource with a first seen ASN of {{device_ip_asnOrg}} since the baseline period.

FIRST-S00048.description_expression: Azure Device Code authentication can be utilized in phishing attacks. This specific rule looks for a user performing device code authentication to an Azure resource for the first time since the baseline period. If this action is not expected, it could be a sign of malicious activity. Examine the event for odd user agent values and look at what other actions the affected account is performing within the Azure estate.
FIRST-S00048.filter_expression:
metadata_vendor = 'Microsoft'
AND metadata_product = "Azure"
AND metadata_deviceEventId = "SignInLogs"
AND lower(fields['properties.authenticationProtocol']) = "devicecode"
FIRST-S00048.name: First Seen Azure Device Code Authentication from User
FIRST-S00048.name_expression: First Seen Azure Device Code Authentication from User
FIRST-S00048.normalized_description: Azure Device Code authentication can be utilized in phishing attacks. This specific rule looks for a user performing device code authentication to an Azure resource for the first time since the baseline period. If this action is not expected, it could be a sign of malicious activity. Examine the event for odd user agent values and look at what other actions the affected account is performing within the Azure estate.
FIRST-S00048.normalized_signal_name: First Seen Azure Device Code Authentication from User
FIRST-S00048.normalized_summary: First Seen Azure Device Code Authentication from {{user_username}}
FIRST-S00048.summary_expression: First Seen Azure Device Code Authentication from {{user_username}}

FIRST-S00058.description_expression: Threat actors may use the vssadmin utility to create volume shadow copies on a Windows operating system and retrieve the Active Directory database (NTDS.dit) file in order to extract credential material. This activity should be treated as high priority if not performed by an authorized systems administrator as part of normal and planned systems maintenance. If this activity is performed as part of normal system maintenance, the rule can be tuned to exclude these groups of users.
FIRST-S00058.filter_expression:
metadata_vendor = 'Microsoft'
AND metadata_product = 'Windows'
AND lower(commandLine) matches /(vssadmin)/
FIRST-S00058.name: First Seen vssadmin command From User
FIRST-S00058.normalized_description: Threat actors may use the vssadmin utility to create volume shadow copies on a Windows operating system and retrieve the Active Directory database (NTDS.dit) file in order to extract credential material. This activity should be treated as high priority if not performed by an authorized systems administrator as part of normal and planned systems maintenance. If this activity is performed as part of normal system maintenance, the rule can be tuned to exclude these groups of users.
FIRST-S00058.normalized_summary: First Seen vssadmin command by User: {{user_username}} on the Host: {{device_hostname}}
FIRST-S00058.summary_expression: First Seen vssadmin command by User: {{user_username}} on the Host: {{device_hostname}}

FIRST-S00059.description_expression: Threat actors may use the esentutl utility to create volume shadow copies and/or backups on a Windows operating system and retrieve the Active Directory database (NTDS.dit) file in order to extract credential material. Esentutl can also be utilized to download files from a remote share or URL. This activity should be treated as high priority if not performed by an authorized systems administrator as part of normal and planned systems maintenance.
FIRST-S00059.filter_expression:
metadata_vendor = 'Microsoft'
AND metadata_product = 'Windows'
AND lower(commandLine) matches /(esentutl)/
FIRST-S00059.name: First Seen esentutl command From User
FIRST-S00059.normalized_description: Threat actors may use the esentutl utility to create volume shadow copies and/or backups on a Windows operating system and retrieve the Active Directory database (NTDS.dit) file in order to extract credential material. This activity should be treated as high priority if not performed by an authorized systems administrator as part of normal and planned systems maintenance.

FIRST-S00059.summary_expression: First Seen wbadmin command by User: {{user_username}} on Host: {{device_hostname}}

FIRST-S00061.description_expression: This signal looks for a new removable USB device name being used by a host not seen since the baseline period. This activity by itself is not necessarily malicious, but can be indicative of potential lateral movement or initial access tactics. If the device name is unexpected and not authorized to be used in the environment, investigate the alert further and look for file creation events to the drive in question. The fields["EventData.DeviceDescription"] field contains the device name.
FIRST-S00061.filter_expression:
metadata_vendor = "Microsoft"
and metadata_deviceEventId = "Security-6416"
and fields["EventData.ClassName"] = "WPD"
and fields["EventData.DeviceDescription"] !matches /^\w\:\\/
FIRST-S00061.name: First Seen USB device in use on Windows host
FIRST-S00061.name_expression: First Seen USB device in use on Windows host: {{device_hostname}}
FIRST-S00061.normalized_description: This signal looks for a new removable USB device name being used by a host not seen since the baseline period. This activity by itself is not necessarily malicious, but can be indicative of potential lateral movement or initial access tactics. If the device name is unexpected and not authorized to be used in the environment, investigate the alert further and look for file creation events to the drive in question. The fields["EventData.DeviceDescription"] field contains the device name.
FIRST-S00061.normalized_signal_name: First Seen USB device in use on Windows host: {{device_hostname}}
FIRST-S00061.normalized_summary: A First Seen USB device found on {{device_hostname}}
FIRST-S00061.summary_expression: A First Seen USB device found on {{device_hostname}}

FIRST-S00062.description_expression: This alert looks at Windows Filtering Platform Events and flags when a first seen IP address connects to the certificate services process. This can be indictive of enumeration of certificate templates which can potentially lead to forged certificates and privilege escalation avenues. Pivot off the source IP and ensure that this device is authorized to be on the network. In addition, look for other CSE signals related to Active Directory Certificate Services to look for any potentially vulnerable certificate templates being exploited.
FIRST-S00062.filter_expression:
metadata_vendor = "Microsoft" 
and metadata_deviceEventId = "Security-5156"
and application matches /(certsrv\.exe)/
FIRST-S00062.name: First Seen IP Address Connecting to Active Directory Certificate Services Process
FIRST-S00062.name_expression: First Seen IP Address Connecting to Active Directory Certificate Services Process from {{srcDevice_ip}} on {{device_hostname}}
FIRST-S00062.normalized_description: This alert looks at Windows Filtering Platform Events and flags when a first seen IP address connects to the certificate services process. This can be indictive of enumeration of certificate templates which can potentially lead to forged certificates and privilege escalation avenues. Pivot off the source IP and ensure that this device is authorized to be on the network. In addition, look for other CSE signals related to Active Directory Certificate Services to look for any potentially vulnerable certificate templates being exploited.
FIRST-S00062.normalized_signal_name: First Seen IP Address Connecting to Active Directory Certificate Services Process from {{srcDevice_ip}} on {{device_hostname}}
FIRST-S00062.normalized_summary: First Seen IP Address Connecting to Active Directory Certificate Services Process on {{device_hostname}}
FIRST-S00062.summary_expression: First Seen IP Address Connecting to Active Directory Certificate Services Process on {{device_hostname}}

FIRST-S00064.description_expression: This alert looks for a first seen certtificate thumbprint being used to authenticate to an Active Directory environment, resulting in a Kerberos ticket being successfully issued. This alert is designed to catch Active Directory Certificate Services related attacks, ensure the certificate thumprint is valid, correlate the thumbprint ID with other Certificate Services events, particularly looking for recently issued templates.
FIRST-S00064.filter_expression:
metadata_vendor = "Microsoft"
and metadata_deviceEventId = "Security-4768"
and NOT fields["EventData.CertThumbprint"] = "null"
and success = true
FIRST-S00064.name: First Seen Certificate Thumbprint in Successful Kerberos Authentication
FIRST-S00064.name_expression: First Seen Certificate Thumbprint in Successful Kerberos Authentication on {{device_hostname}}
FIRST-S00064.normalized_description: This alert looks for a first seen certtificate thumbprint being used to authenticate to an Active Directory environment, resulting in a Kerberos ticket being successfully issued. This alert is designed to catch Active Directory Certificate Services related attacks, ensure the certificate thumprint is valid, correlate the thumbprint ID with other Certificate Services events, particularly looking for recently issued templates.
FIRST-S00064.normalized_signal_name: First Seen Certificate Thumbprint in Successful Kerberos Authentication on {{device_hostname}}
FIRST-S00064.normalized_summary: First Seen Certificate Thumbprint in Successful Kerberos Authentication on {{device_hostname}} by {{user_username}}
FIRST-S00064.summary_expression: First Seen Certificate Thumbprint in Successful Kerberos Authentication on {{device_hostname}} by {{user_username}}

FIRST-S00065.commit_message: TLAB-1077 Remove Silent Mode name from FS65 and delete disabled by default version
FIRST-S00065.description_expression: First Seen rule which triggers when there are at least two successful logins from the same user with different country codes indicating possible credential theft. It is recommended to add filtering criteria to the expression to reduce false positives, such as known VPN addresses.(If degradation issues occur it is recommendation implementing tuning around your expected network)
FIRST-S00065.filter_expression:
objectType = 'Authentication'
AND normalizedAction IN ('logon','domainLogon')
AND success = true
AND srcDevice_ip_isInternal = false
FIRST-S00065.name: First Seen Successful Authentication From Unexpected Country
FIRST-S00065.name_expression: First Seen Successful Authentication From Unexpected Country: {{srcDevice_ip_countryCode}} for User: {{user_username}}
FIRST-S00065.normalized_description: First Seen rule which triggers when there are at least two successful logins from the same user with different country codes indicating possible credential theft. It is recommended to add filtering criteria to the expression to reduce false positives, such as known VPN addresses.(If degradation issues occur it is recommendation implementing tuning around your expected network)

FIRST-S00065.normalized_summary: First Seen successful authentication From unexpected country for user: {{user_username}}
FIRST-S00065.summary_expression: First Seen successful authentication From unexpected country for user: {{user_username}}

FIRST-S00066.description_expression: This signal looks for a first seen user requesting an export of an Okta report. The various Okta report types can be found in the “Reports” section of the Okta administrative portal and can include various report types such as application password help, MFA usage and reports around user access. During the October 2023 Okta incident, threat actors downloaded reports from Okta portals to extract information regarding user contact information. Ensure that the user that is requesting such reports is authorized and that this activity is expected. If a suspicious report generation event occurs, look at the “target” element within the event to gain more detailed information as to the type of report being generated and exported.
FIRST-S00066.name: Okta - First Seen User Requesting Report
FIRST-S00066.name_expression: First Seen user: {{user_username}} requesting Okta report
FIRST-S00066.normalized_description: This signal looks for a first seen user requesting an export of an Okta report. The various Okta report types can be found in the “Reports” section of the Okta administrative portal and can include various report types such as application password help, MFA usage and reports around user access. During the October 2023 Okta incident, threat actors downloaded reports from Okta portals to extract information regarding user contact information. Ensure that the user that is requesting such reports is authorized and that this activity is expected. If a suspicious report generation event occurs, look at the “target” element within the event to gain more detailed information as to the type of report being generated and exported.
FIRST-S00066.normalized_signal_name: First Seen user: {{user_username}} requesting Okta report
FIRST-S00066.normalized_summary: First Seen user: {{user_username}} requesting Okta report from IP address: {{srcDevice_ip}}
FIRST-S00066.summary_expression: First Seen user: {{user_username}} requesting Okta report from IP address: {{srcDevice_ip}}

FIRST-S00067.commit_message: TLAB-2373: change mappings to srcDevice_ip for Okta logs. Exclude NULL srcDevice_ip from FIRST-S00067 rule expression (#5898)
FIRST-S00067.description_expression: This signal looks for a new Client ID value ( mapped to the user_username field ) and ASN combination being issued an OIDC token, excluding the Okta Browser Plugin and Okta Dashboard. Use the Okta admin portal and look at the "Applications" section to cross-reference the Client ID value - ensure that the IP address that is requesting the token is known and that this operation is expected and authorized.
FIRST-S00067.filter_expression:
metadata_vendor = 'Okta'
and metadata_deviceEventId = "app.oauth2.token.grant.access_token"
and success = true
and fields['actor.displayName'] != "Okta Browser Plugin"
and fields['actor.displayName'] != "Okta Dashboard"
and srcDevice_ip_asnNumber IS NOT NULL
FIRST-S00067.name: Okta - First Seen Client ID/ASN combo in successful OIDC token grant
FIRST-S00067.name_expression: Okta - First Seen Client ID/ASN combo in successful OIDC token grant for{{user_username}} from ASN {{srcDevice_ip_asnNumber}}
FIRST-S00067.normalized_description: This signal looks for a new Client ID value ( mapped to the user_username field ) and ASN combination being issued an OIDC token, excluding the Okta Browser Plugin and Okta Dashboard. Use the Okta admin portal and look at the "Applications" section to cross-reference the Client ID value - ensure that the IP address that is requesting the token is known and that this operation is expected and authorized.
FIRST-S00067.normalized_signal_name: Okta - First Seen Client ID/ASN combo in successful OIDC token grant for{{user_username}} from ASN {{srcDevice_ip_asnNumber}}
FIRST-S00067.normalized_summary: Okta - First Seen Client ID in successful OIDC token grant for Client ID:{{user_username}} from {{device_ip}}
FIRST-S00067.summary_expression: Okta - First Seen Client ID in successful OIDC token grant for Client ID:{{user_username}} from {{device_ip}}

FIRST-S00068.description_expression: A user not seen since the baseline period has accessed the Okta admin application. Ensure that this user is expected to perform Okta administrative activities. If this user is expected and authroized, consider adding the user to the "Okta_Admins" match list to exclude the user from this signal.
FIRST-S00068.filter_expression:
metadata_vendor = 'Okta'
AND metadata_deviceEventId = 'user.session.access_admin_app'
AND success = true
AND NOT array_contains(listMatches, 'Okta_Admins')
FIRST-S00068.name: Okta - First Seen User Accessing Admin Application
FIRST-S00068.name_expression: Okta - First Seen User:{{user_username}}  has successfully accessed the Okta Admin Application

If this user is expected and authroized, consider adding the user to the "Okta_Admins" match list to exclude the user from this signal.
FIRST-S00068.normalized_signal_name: Okta - First Seen User:{{user_username}}  has successfully accessed the Okta Admin Application
FIRST-S00068.normalized_summary: First Seen User ( {{user_username}} )  Accessing Okta Admin Application
FIRST-S00068.summary_expression: First Seen User ( {{user_username}} )  Accessing Okta Admin Application

Ensure that access of this application is expected and authorized, look for other Okta events around the user account in question to determine whether access to this application is expected and authorized.
FIRST-S00070.name: Okta - First Seen Application Accessed by User

FIRST-S00070.summary_expression: {{user_username}} has an accessed an Okta application not seen since the baseline period.

FIRST-S00071.description_expression: First observance of a user logging on to the Amazon AWS console. This could be indicative of new administrator onboarding, or an unauthorized access to the AWS console. Recommended to investigate the nature of the user account and the login.
FIRST-S00071.filter_expression:
objectType = 'Authentication'
AND metadata_vendor = 'Amazon AWS'
AND action = 'ConsoleLogin'
AND success = true
FIRST-S00071.name: First Seen AWS ConsoleLogin by User
FIRST-S00071.name_expression: First Seen AWS ConsoleLogin by User: {{user_username}}

FIRST-S00072.description_expression: `This detection is a first observed execution of Windows process or PowerShell commands that can be run by users or administrators in order to gather password policy and other types of system information in an enterprise environment. The detections in this signal are based off variations found in Atomic Red Team test cases. Reference:  https://github.com/redcanaryco/atomic-red-team/blob/master/atomics/T1615/T1615.md. Look at the command line and parent process details of the signal in order to determine if this execution is legitimate or part of system provisioning or systems administration operations.
FIRST-S00072.name: First Seen Group Policy Discovery Operation
FIRST-S00072.name_expression: First Seen Group Policy Discovery by {{user_username}}
FIRST-S00072.normalized_description: `This detection is a first observed execution of Windows process or PowerShell commands that can be run by users or administrators in order to gather password policy and other types of system information in an enterprise environment. The detections in this signal are based off variations found in Atomic Red Team test cases. Reference:  https://github.com/redcanaryco/atomic-red-team/blob/master/atomics/T1615/T1615.md. Look at the command line and parent process details of the signal in order to determine if this execution is legitimate or part of system provisioning or systems administration operations.
FIRST-S00072.normalized_signal_name: First Seen Group Policy Discovery by {{user_username}}
FIRST-S00072.normalized_summary: First Seen Group Policy Discovery on host: {{device_hostname}} by User: {{user_username}}
FIRST-S00072.summary_expression: First Seen Group Policy Discovery on host: {{device_hostname}} by User: {{user_username}}

FIRST-S00073.description_expression: The first observed execution of the PowerShell CMDLet Get-ADDefaultDomainPasswordPolicy on this host, this CMDLet can be used in the discovery of Windows Domain Password Policies by threat actors. Investigating the host and active users for additional activity around the time of execution is recommended.
FIRST-S00073.name: First Seen Get-ADDefaultDomainPasswordPolicy
FIRST-S00073.normalized_description: The first observed execution of the PowerShell CMDLet Get-ADDefaultDomainPasswordPolicy on this host, this CMDLet can be used in the discovery of Windows Domain Password Policies by threat actors. Investigating the host and active users for additional activity around the time of execution is recommended.
FIRST-S00073.normalized_summary: The PowerShell CMDLet Get-ADDefaultDomainPasswordPolicy has been observed being executed on this host.
FIRST-S00073.summary_expression: The PowerShell CMDLet Get-ADDefaultDomainPasswordPolicy has been observed being executed on this host.

FIRST-S00074.description_expression: First observed execution of the driverquery command on the following device host: {{device_hostname}}. Driverquery is a useful command for an attacker to enumerate local device drivers to determine next steps in the attack. Vulnerability scanners and automated processes may trigger this detection, it is recommended to identify and filter these processes out using a Tuning Expression for the corresponding baseImage. On detection, investigating the host and process executing the command to assist in understanding the context of the execution.
FIRST-S00074.name: First Seen driverquery execution on host
FIRST-S00074.normalized_description: First observed execution of the driverquery command on the following device host: {{device_hostname}}. Driverquery is a useful command for an attacker to enumerate local device drivers to determine next steps in the attack. Vulnerability scanners and automated processes may trigger this detection, it is recommended to identify and filter these processes out using a Tuning Expression for the corresponding baseImage. On detection, investigating the host and process executing the command to assist in understanding the context of the execution.
FIRST-S00074.normalized_summary: The command driverquery was performed by {{user_username}} on {{device_hostname}}
FIRST-S00074.summary_expression: The command driverquery was performed by {{user_username}} on {{device_hostname}}

FIRST-S00075.description_expression: This is the first observance of an account’s activity in the Amazon AWS System Manager Session Manager (SSM). This could be indicative of administrative activity or unauthorized access to the AWS EC2 instance management interface. It is recommended to investigate the account and activity to ensure it is authorized.
FIRST-S00075.filter_expression:
metadata_vendor = 'Amazon AWS'
AND metadata_product = 'CloudTrail'
AND application = 'ssm.amazonaws.com'
AND NOT user_username_role RLIKE 'awsservicerolefor(trustedadvisor|amazonssm|instancesquicksetup|resourceexplorer|inspector)'
AND NOT user_username_role LIKE '%quicksetup%'
FIRST-S00075.name: First Seen AWS SSM activity by User
FIRST-S00075.name_expression: First Seen AWS SSM activity by User: {{user_username}}
FIRST-S00075.normalized_description: This is the first observance of an account’s activity in the Amazon AWS System Manager Session Manager (SSM). This could be indicative of administrative activity or unauthorized access to the AWS EC2 instance management interface. It is recommended to investigate the account and activity to ensure it is authorized.
FIRST-S00075.normalized_signal_name: First Seen AWS SSM activity by User: {{user_username}}
FIRST-S00075.normalized_summary: First Seen AWS ConsoleLogin by {{user_username}}  performing {{action}}
FIRST-S00075.summary_expression: First Seen AWS ConsoleLogin by {{user_username}}  performing {{action}}

FIRST-S00076.description_expression: Microsoft’s Net.exe can be used for multiple Discovery tactics, including Password Policy, Permissions, Account and Domain Trust Discovery. This detection identifies the first observance of a Net related command on a system related to these discovery tactics. It is recommended to investigate the host and user to determine if this is authorized admin activity or needs further inspection.
FIRST-S00076.filter_expression:
(
 lower(baseImage) like '%\net.exe' OR lower(baseImage) like '%\net1.exe'
)
 AND 
(
 lower(commandLine) like '%group%' 
 OR lower(commandLine) like '%localgroup%'
 OR lower(commandLine) like '%accounts%' 
 OR lower(commandLine) like '%use%' 
 OR lower(commandLine) like '%stop%'
 OR lower(commandLine) like '%domain%computers%' 
 OR lower(commandLine) like '%domain%admins%'
 OR lower(commandLine) like '%administrators%'
)
AND NOT array_contains(listMatches,'vuln_scanners')
AND user_username is not null
FIRST-S00076.name: First Seen Net Command Use on Host
FIRST-S00076.name_expression: First Seen net command use on host: {{device_hostname}}
FIRST-S00076.normalized_description: Microsoft’s Net.exe can be used for multiple Discovery tactics, including Password Policy, Permissions, Account and Domain Trust Discovery. This detection identifies the first observance of a Net related command on a system related to these discovery tactics. It is recommended to investigate the host and user to determine if this is authorized admin activity or needs further inspection.
FIRST-S00076.normalized_signal_name: First Seen net command use on host: {{device_hostname}}
FIRST-S00076.normalized_summary: First observance of net command use on this Host: {{device_hostname}} by the account: {{user_username}}
FIRST-S00076.summary_expression: First observance of net command use on this Host: {{device_hostname}} by the account: {{user_username}}

FIRST-S00081.description_expression: A first seen model id was observed in AWS Bedrock. The PutFoundationModelEntitlement API call grants permission to put entitlement to access a foundational model. Ensure this model is authorized to be utilized in the environment and that the user requesting access to the model is authorized to perform these actions.
FIRST-S00081.filter_expression:
metadata_vendor = "Amazon AWS"
AND metadata_product = "CloudTrail"
AND application = "bedrock.amazonaws.com"
AND action = "PutFoundationModelEntitlement"
AND fields["responseElements.status"] = "SUCCESS"

FIRST-S00082.description_expression: A first seen user was observed enumerating AWS Bedrock models via the ListFoundationModels API call. Ensure that the user performing the enumeration is authorized to work within AWS Bedrock. The http_userAgent field will contain the user agent used to perform this enumeration and will help determine whether a browser or CLI tool was used to perform this type of enumeration. Consider excluding service accounts and authorized users from this rule via a rule tuning expression if excessive signal activity is observed.
FIRST-S00082.filter_expression:
metadata_vendor = "Amazon AWS"
AND metadata_product = "CloudTrail"
AND application = "bedrock.amazonaws.com"
AND action = "ListFoundationModels"

FIRST-S00085.description_expression: An AWS Bedrock Agent has been created in the environment by a Role seen for the first time since the baseline period. If this role is not expected in the environment and was not originally assigned IAM rights to Bedrock, this activity could be indicative of privilege escalation. Bedrock Agents can be configured with various parameters to build AI applications. Take a look at the "responseElements.agent.agentName" field to see the name of the agent being created. Ensure that the user creating the agent is authorized to develop AI applications within the environment.
FIRST-S00085.filter_expression:
cloud_provider = "AWS"
AND metadata_vendor = "Amazon AWS"
AND metadata_product = 'CloudTrail'
AND application = "bedrock.amazonaws.com"
AND action = "CreateAgent"

Bedrock Agents can be configured with various parameters to build AI applications. Take a look at the "responseElements.agent.agentName" field to see the name of the agent being created. Ensure that the user creating the agent is authorized to develop AI applications within the environment.

FIRST-S00086.description_expression: Trufflehog is a tool that can be utilized to find and verify secrets. When Trufflehog locates AWS credentials, it attempts to validate them using the GetCallerIdentity API call. This signal looks for the default Trufflehog User Agent within CloudTrail telemetry, combined with the GetCallerIdentity API call occurring from an IP address not seen since the baseline period. This signal is designed to pair with “Trufflehog AWS Credential Verification Detected” to provide coverage for legitimate and internal Trufflehog scans. Within this telemetry, the user_username field will contain the value of the username associated with the secret or credential that Trufflehog is attempting to verify. Look at the events surrounding the source IP address of this event. Look for any potential areas that may have contained keys or secrets for the user_username value.
FIRST-S00086.filter_expression:
metadata_vendor = "Amazon AWS"
and metadata_product = "CloudTrail"
and lower(action) = "getcalleridentity"
and lower(http_userAgent) = "trufflehog"
FIRST-S00086.name: First Seen IP Address Performing Trufflehog AWS Credential Verification
FIRST-S00086.name_expression: First Seen IP Address Performing Trufflehog AWS Credential Verification
FIRST-S00086.normalized_description: Trufflehog is a tool that can be utilized to find and verify secrets. This signal looks for the default Trufflehog User Agent within CloudTrail telemetry, combined with the GetCallerIdentity API call occurring from an IP address not seen since the baseline period. This signal is designed to pair with “Trufflehog AWS Credential Verification Detected” to provide coverage for legitimate and internal Trufflehog scans. Within this telemetry, the user_username field will contain the value of the username associated with the secret or credential that Trufflehog is attempting to verify. Look at the events surrounding the source IP address of this event. Look for any potential areas that may have contained keys or secrets for the user_username value.
FIRST-S00086.normalized_signal_name: First Seen IP Address Performing Trufflehog AWS Credential Verification
FIRST-S00086.normalized_summary: First Seen IP Address: {{srcDevice_ip}}  Performing Trufflehog AWS Credential Verification
FIRST-S00086.summary_expression: First Seen IP Address: {{srcDevice_ip}}  Performing Trufflehog AWS Credential Verification

FIRST-S00087.description_expression: AWS EC2 launch templates allows cloud administrators to specify instance configuration information in a templated format. Granting permissions to modify or create launch templates within EC2 in certain circumstances grants the user PassRole permissions, potentially opening privilege escalation avenues via IAM. The following AWS documentation outlines this behavior: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/permissions-for-launch-templates.html. Look at other events the user in question is performing in order to investigate this signal. Consider excluding authorized users via a match list if this signal is triggering too many false positives.
FIRST-S00087.filter_expression:
metadata_vendor = "Amazon AWS"
and application = "ec2.amazonaws.com"
and (action = "CreateLaunchTemplate" OR action = "ModifyLaunchTemplate")
FIRST-S00087.name: First Seen User Creating or Modifying EC2 Launch Template
FIRST-S00087.name_expression: First Seen User Creating or Modifying EC2 Launch Template

Look at other events the user in question is performing in order to investigate this signal. FIRST-S00087.normalized_summary: First Seen User: {{user_username}}  Creating or Modifying EC2 Launch Template
FIRST-S00087.summary_expression: First Seen User: {{user_username}}  Creating or Modifying EC2 Launch Template

FIRST-S00088.description_expression: A user has performed NTLM authentication to a host on the network for the first time since the baseline period has been established. Rule is disabled by default and requires tuning. Tuning guidance: depending on configurations in the environment, NTLM authentication may be necessary and expected. Examine the username, source IP and destination host in order to ensure that this activity is expected. Look at other signals around the entity in question to determine if any other type of suspicious activity may have occurred. It may also be prudent to isolate the rule to sensitive users and groups
FIRST-S00088.filter_expression:
metadata_vendor = "Microsoft"
and metadata_deviceEventId = "Security-4624"
and fields["EventData.AuthenticationPackageName"] = "NTLM"
and user_username != "anonymous logon"
FIRST-S00088.name: First Seen NTLM Authentication to Host (User)
FIRST-S00088.name_expression: First Seen User Performing NTLM Authentication to Host
FIRST-S00088.normalized_description: A user has performed NTLM authentication to a host on the network for the first time since the baseline period has been established. Examine the username, source IP and destination host in order to ensure that this activity is expected. Look at other signals around the entity in question to determine if any other type of suspicious activity may have occurred. It may also be prudent to isolate the rule to sensitive users and groups

FIRST-S00090.commit_message: Added logging requirements of GitHub Enterprise to the description of GitHub rules released 6 Dec 2024
FIRST-S00090.description_expression: Detects new application usage of the GitHub API. New applications utilizing the API may be routine, however this may also reveal malicious applications utilzing the API. This detection was developed utilizing GitHub Enterprise Audit logging. Refer to https://help.sumologic.com/docs/integrations/app-development/github/#collecting-logs-for-github for instructions on ingesting GitHub Enterprise Audit logs.
FIRST-S00090.filter_expression:
metadata_product = 'GitHub Enterprise Audit'
AND action = 'api.request'
AND application IS NOT NULL

FIRST-S00092.description_expression:
Context:
Creation or modification of release pipelines provide opportunities for the attacker to introduce malicious code into the release process.
Detection:
This detection monitors for users creating a release pipeline for the first time after the baseline period (by default, 90 days).
Recommended Actions:
Investigate the pipeline that has been created for malicious content and the user account activity for signs of account compromise or insider threat activity.
Tuning Recommendations:
Maintain a match list of users known and approved to create Azure DevOps pipeline and add it as a tuning expression for the rule.
Credits:
This detection is based on the Azure Sentinel ADOPipelineModifiedbyNewUser.yaml rule.
FIRST-S00092.filter_expression:
metadata_vendor = "Microsoft"
AND metadata_product = "Azure DevOps Auditing"
AND metadata_deviceEventId = "AzureDevOpsAuditEvent"
AND action = "Release.ReleasePipelineCreated"
FIRST-S00092.name: Azure DevOps - First Seen User Creating Release Pipeline
FIRST-S00092.name_expression: Azure DevOps - First Seen User Creating Release Pipeline
FIRST-S00092.normalized_description:

Detection:
This detection monitors for users creating a release pipeline for the first time after the baseline period (by default, 90 days).
