MATCH-S00886.commit_message: Update test cases and chmod rule
MATCH-S00886.description_expression: This alert looks for a "chmod" execution on a file that is found on the /tmp directory of a Linux or macOS host. Threat actors may download and copy files to this directory and add execution bits or change permissions on these files.
MATCH-S00886.expression:
baseImage = "/bin/chmod"
AND (lower(fields["exec_env.env.PWD"]) LIKE "/tmp%"
OR lower(commandLine) LIKE "%/tmp/%")
MATCH-S00886.name: Suspicious chmod Execution
MATCH-S00886.normalized_description: This alert looks for a "chmod" execution on a file that is found on the /tmp directory of a Linux or macOS host. Threat actors may download and copy files to this directory and add execution bits or change permissions on these files.

MATCH-S00887.description_expression: A local port has been forwarded and made available for external connectivity utilizing the Visual Studio Code port forwarding feature. Ensure that this activity is expected and approved, as it may be used by developers for legitimate purposes. This activity could potentially circumvent existing egress/ingress controls by exposing local services to the internet. Look for other events around this time frame in order to ascertain the purpose for the port forwarding activities. If tuning of the rule is required, create a list that contains developers who utilize this functionality and exclude it from the expression.
MATCH-S00887.expression:
objectClassification = "Network"
and (lower(baseImage) matches /(code\-tunnel)/ OR lower(application) matches /(code\-tunnel)/)
and (
        lower(baseImage) matches /(visual studio code\.app)/ OR
        lower(baseImage) matches /(microsoft vs code)/ OR
        lower(application) matches /(microsoft vs code)/
    )
MATCH-S00887.name: Port Forwarding Enabled via Visual Studio Code
MATCH-S00887.name_expression: Port Forwarding Enabled via Visual Studio Code on {{device_hostname}} by {{user_username}}
MATCH-S00887.normalized_description: A local port has been forwarded and made available for external connectivity utilizing the Visual Studio Code port forwarding feature. Ensure that this activity is expected and approved, as it may be used by developers for legitimate purposes. This activity could potentially circumvent existing egress/ingress controls by exposing local services to the internet. Look for other events around this time frame in order to ascertain the purpose for the port forwarding activities. If tuning of the rule is required, create a list that contains developers who utilize this functionality and exclude it from the expression.
MATCH-S00887.normalized_signal_name: Port Forwarding Enabled via Visual Studio Code on {{device_hostname}} by {{user_username}}
MATCH-S00887.normalized_summary: Port Forwarding Enabled via Visual Studio Code on {{device_hostname}} by {{user_username}}
MATCH-S00887.summary_expression: Port Forwarding Enabled via Visual Studio Code on {{device_hostname}} by {{user_username}}

MATCH-S00888.commit_message: Add Stream Field Back to Rules
MATCH-S00888.description_expression: Microsoft Teams External Access has been enabled, this settings allows any users that are external to your Teams/Office organization to message users that are within your Teams/Office organization. If this setting change is unplanned or unexpected it is recommended that this activity be reviewed.  MIcrosoft Teams provides administrators the ability to allow only specific external domains to message users within the organization. Look for Office 365 events with the "MessageSent" or "MemberAdded" event names in order to gain more detail as to what users were invited to which Teams channels - if any.
MATCH-S00888.expression:
metadata_product = "Office 365"
and action = "TeamsAdminAction"
and lower(commandLine) matches /(tenantfederationsettings\/configuration\/global)/
and fields["ModifiedProperties.1.Name"] = "AllowFederatedUsers"
and fields["ModifiedProperties.1.NewValue"] = true
MATCH-S00888.name: Microsoft Teams External Access Enabled
MATCH-S00888.name_expression: Microsoft Teams External Access Enabled by {{user_UserId}}
MATCH-S00888.normalized_description: Microsoft Teams External Access has been enabled, this settings allows any users that are external to your Teams/Office organization to message users that are within your Teams/Office organization. If this setting change is unplanned or unexpected it is recommended that this activity be reviewed.  MIcrosoft Teams provides administrators the ability to allow only specific external domains to message users within the organization. Look for Office 365 events with the "MessageSent" or "MemberAdded" event names in order to gain more detail as to what users were invited to which Teams channels - if any.
MATCH-S00888.normalized_signal_name: Microsoft Teams External Access Enabled by {{user_UserId}}
MATCH-S00888.normalized_summary: Microsoft Team External Access has been enabled by {{user_UserId}}
MATCH-S00888.summary_expression: Microsoft Team External Access has been enabled by {{user_UserId}}

MATCH-S00894.description_expression: HAR files contain session telemetry and network traffic. These file types are typically generated using the "developer tools" options on modern browsers like Chrome, Edge or Firefox. These files may contain various sensitive data such as session keys, tokens or cookies which may be extracted by a threat actor in order to access systems  which the keys, tokens or cookies in the HAR files have access to. Ensure that this operation is expected and ensure to sanitize the HAR file of any sensitive credential material.
MATCH-S00894.expression:
action = "FileCreate"
AND ( 
    lower(changeTarget) matches /(^.*\.(har)$)/
    OR
    lower(file_path) matches /(^.*\.(har)$)/
)
MATCH-S00894.name: HAR file creation observed on host
MATCH-S00894.normalized_description: HAR files contain session telemetry and network traffic. These file types are typically generated using the "developer tools" options on modern browsers like Chrome, Edge or Firefox. These files may contain various sensitive data such as session keys, tokens or cookies which may be extracted by a threat actor in order to access systems  which the keys, tokens or cookies in the HAR files have access to. Ensure that this operation is expected and ensure to sanitize the HAR file of any sensitive credential material.
MATCH-S00894.normalized_summary: {{user_username}} has created a HAR file on {{device_hostname}}
MATCH-S00894.summary_expression: {{user_username}} has created a HAR file on {{device_hostname}}

MATCH-S00895.description_expression: NinjaCopy is a legacy PowerShell tool that can copy files from an NTFS volume in a manner that bypasses SACL auditing as well as DACL controls such as only allowing SYSTEM to open a file. This tool is often used to exfiltrate the Active Directory database (NTDS.dit) file from domain controllers in order to extract credential material. Usage of this tool in an environment should be considered highly suspciious.
MATCH-S00895.name: NinjaCopy Usage Detected
MATCH-S00895.normalized_description: NinjaCopy is a legacy PowerShell tool that can copy files from an NTFS volume in a manner that bypasses SACL auditing as well as DACL controls such as only allowing SYSTEM to open a file. This tool is often used to exfiltrate the Active Directory database (NTDS.dit) file from domain controllers in order to extract credential material. Usage of this tool in an environment should be considered highly suspciious.
MATCH-S00895.normalized_summary: NinjaCopy Usage Detected on {{device_hostname}} from the {{baseImage}} process
MATCH-S00895.summary_expression: NinjaCopy Usage Detected on {{device_hostname}} from the {{baseImage}} process

MATCH-S00896.summary_expression: {{user_username}} has changed an Azure Authentication Policy

MATCH-S00897.description_expression: Threat actors may install browser extensions as a form of persistence on victim systems. Look up the 32 character extension ID in order to ensure that the extension is valid and expected to be installed as part of normal business operations. This extension ID can be found in the following values: {{file_path}} and/or {{changeTarget}} depending on the source of the telemetry. This rule logic utilizes Sysmon file creation events, which need to be enabled and configured on relevant assets.
MATCH-S00897.expression:
action = "FileCreate"
and lower(changeTarget) matches /local extension settings\\\w{32}$/
and lower(baseImage) matches /\\application\\(chrome\.exe|msedge\.exe)/
MATCH-S00897.name: Chromium Extension Installed
MATCH-S00897.name_expression: A Chromium Extension was installed on {{device_hostname}} by {{user_username}}
MATCH-S00897.normalized_description: Threat actors may install browser extensions as a form of persistence on victim systems. Look up the 32 character extension ID in order to ensure that the extension is valid and expected to be installed as part of normal business operations. This extension ID can be found in the following values: {{file_path}} and/or {{changeTarget}} depending on the source of the telemetry. This rule logic utilizes Sysmon file creation events, which need to be enabled and configured on relevant assets.
MATCH-S00897.normalized_signal_name: A Chromium Extension was installed on {{device_hostname}} by {{user_username}}
MATCH-S00897.normalized_summary: A Chromium Extension was installed on {{device_hostname}} by {{user_username}}  with the following path: {{file_path}}
MATCH-S00897.summary_expression: A Chromium Extension was installed on {{device_hostname}} by {{user_username}}  with the following path: {{file_path}}

MATCH-S00898.description_expression: This alert looks at Active Directory Certificate Services Auditing Events to look for a certificate template issued that allows all domain users the ability to enroll the template. Combined with other misconfigurations within a certificate template, this can potentially allow a threat actor to turn a certificate obtained from such a vulnerable template into a Kerberos Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT) which could enable privilege escalation paths.
MATCH-S00898.expression:
metadata_vendor = "Microsoft" and
metadata_deviceEventId = "Security-4898"
and lower(fields["EventData.SecurityDescriptor"]) matches /(allow.*domain users.*\n.enroll)/
MATCH-S00898.name: Potentially Misconfigured Active Directory Certificate Template Loaded
MATCH-S00898.name_expression: Potentially Misconfigured Active Directory Certificate Template Loaded on {{device_hostname}}
MATCH-S00898.normalized_description: This alert looks at Active Directory Certificate Services Auditing Events to look for a certificate template issued that allows all domain users the ability to enroll the template. Combined with other misconfigurations within a certificate template, this can potentially allow a threat actor to turn a certificate obtained from such a vulnerable template into a Kerberos Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT) which could enable privilege escalation paths.
MATCH-S00898.normalized_signal_name: Potentially Misconfigured Active Directory Certificate Template Loaded on {{device_hostname}}
MATCH-S00898.normalized_summary: Potentially Misconfigured Active Directory Certificate Template Loaded on {{device_hostname}}
MATCH-S00898.summary_expression: Potentially Misconfigured Active Directory Certificate Template Loaded on {{device_hostname}}

MATCH-S00899.description_expression: This alert looks for an Active Directory certificate being modified with the "Any Purpose" OID. Threat actors may abuse this misconfiguration to forge certificates and Kerberos Ticket Granting Tickets (TGT). Specifically, this misconfiguration can exploit the "ESC2" attack path via https://posts.specterops.io/certified-pre-owned-d95910965cd2. If this event is deemed suspicious, look for authentication events (4624) that utilize this certificate template. Also ensure that this change was expected and approved within the environment.
MATCH-S00899.expression:
metadata_vendor = "Microsoft" 
and metadata_deviceEventId = "Security-5136"
and fields["EventData.ObjectClass"] = "pKICertificateTemplate"
and fields["EventData.AttributeValue"] = "2.5.29.37.0"
MATCH-S00899.name: Suspicious Active Directory Certificate Modification
MATCH-S00899.normalized_description: This alert looks for an Active Directory certificate being modified with the "Any Purpose" OID. Threat actors may abuse this misconfiguration to forge certificates and Kerberos Ticket Granting Tickets (TGT). Specifically, this misconfiguration can exploit the "ESC2" attack path via https://posts.specterops.io/certified-pre-owned-d95910965cd2. If this event is deemed suspicious, look for authentication events (4624) that utilize this certificate template. Also ensure that this change was expected and approved within the environment.
MATCH-S00899.normalized_summary: Suspicious Active Directory Certificate Modification by {{user_username}}
MATCH-S00899.summary_expression: Suspicious Active Directory Certificate Modification by {{user_username}}

MATCH-S00900.description_expression: This alert looks at Active Directory Certificate Services Auditing Events to look for a certificate template issued that allows domain users full control over the certificate in addition to allowing an "Any Purpose" Certificate Application Policy. Specifically, this misconfiguration can exploit the "ESC1" attack path via https://posts.specterops.io/certified-pre-owned-d95910965cd2. This combination allows a threat actor to turn a certificate obtained from a vulnerable template into a Kerberos Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT) which could enable privilege escalation paths.
MATCH-S00900.expression:
metadata_vendor = "Microsoft" and
metadata_deviceEventId = "Security-4898"
and fields["EventData.TemplateContent"] matches /(2\.5\.29\.37\.0)/
and lower(fields["EventData.SecurityDescriptor"]) matches /(allow.*domain users.*\n.*full control)/
MATCH-S00900.name: Overly-Permissive Active Directory Certificate Template Loaded
MATCH-S00900.name_expression: Overly-Permissive Active Directory Certificate Template Loaded on {{device_hostname}}
MATCH-S00900.normalized_description: This alert looks at Active Directory Certificate Services Auditing Events to look for a certificate template issued that allows domain users full control over the certificate in addition to allowing an "Any Purpose" Certificate Application Policy. Specifically, this misconfiguration can exploit the "ESC1" attack path via https://posts.specterops.io/certified-pre-owned-d95910965cd2. This combination allows a threat actor to turn a certificate obtained from a vulnerable template into a Kerberos Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT) which could enable privilege escalation paths.
MATCH-S00900.normalized_signal_name: Overly-Permissive Active Directory Certificate Template Loaded on {{device_hostname}}
MATCH-S00900.normalized_summary: Overly-Permissive Active Directory Certificate Template Loaded from {{device_hostname}}
MATCH-S00900.summary_expression: Overly-Permissive Active Directory Certificate Template Loaded from {{device_hostname}}

MATCH-S00901.description_expression: This alert looks at Active Directory Certificate Services Auditing Events to look for a certificate template issued that allows the enrolee to supply a subject and allows all domain users to enroll. This combination allows a threat actor to turn a certificate obtained from a vulnerable template into a Kerberos Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT) which could enable privilege escalation paths. Specifically, this misconfiguration can exploit the "ESC1" attack path via https://posts.specterops.io/certified-pre-owned-d95910965cd2.
MATCH-S00901.expression:
metadata_vendor = "Microsoft" and
metadata_deviceEventId = "Security-4898"
and lower(fields["EventData.TemplateContent"]) matches /(ct_flag_enrollee_supplies_subject....0.1)/
and lower(fields["EventData.SecurityDescriptor"]) matches /(allow.*domain users.*\n.*enroll)/
MATCH-S00901.name: Potentially Vulnerable Active Directory Certificate Services Template Loaded
MATCH-S00901.name_expression: Potentially Vulnerable Active Directory Certificate Services Template Loaded on {{device_hostname}}

This combination allows a threat actor to turn a certificate obtained from a vulnerable template into a Kerberos Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT) which could enable privilege escalation paths. Specifically, this misconfiguration can exploit the "ESC1" attack path via https://posts.specterops.io/certified-pre-owned-d95910965cd2.

MATCH-S00901.normalized_summary: Potentially Vulnerable Active Directory Certificate Services Template Loaded from {{device_hostname}}
MATCH-S00901.summary_expression: Potentially Vulnerable Active Directory Certificate Services Template Loaded from {{device_hostname}}

MATCH-S00902.description_expression: This alert looks for an Active Directory certificate being modified with an Enrollment Agent value that allows an Active Directory principal to enroll a certificate on behalf of another user. Threat actors may abuse this misconfiguration to forge certificates and Kerberos Ticket Granting Tickets (TGT). Specifically, this misconfiguration can exploit the "ESC3" attack path via https://posts.specterops.io/certified-pre-owned-d95910965cd2. If this event is deemed suspicious, look for authentication events (4624) that utilize this certificate template. Also ensure that this change was expected and approved within the environment.
MATCH-S00902.expression:
metadata_vendor = "Microsoft" 
and metadata_deviceEventId = "Security-5136"
and fields["EventData.ObjectClass"] = "pKICertificateTemplate"
and fields["EventData.AttributeValue"] = "1.3.6.1.4.1.311.20.2.1"
and lower(fields["EventData.ObjectDN"]) !matches /(cn\=enrollmentagent\,cn\=certificate templates)/
and lower(fields["EventData.ObjectDN"]) !matches /(cn\=machineenrollmentagent\,cn\=certificate templates)/
and lower(fields["EventData.ObjectDN"]) !matches /(cn\=enrollmentagentoffline\,cn\=certificate templates)/
and lower(fields["EventData.ObjectDN"]) !matches /(cn\=cepencryption\,cn\=certificate templates)/
MATCH-S00902.name: Suspicious Active Directory Certificate Modification - Enrollment Agent
MATCH-S00902.name_expression: Suspicious Active Directory Certificate Modification - Enrollment Agent by {{user_username}}
MATCH-S00902.normalized_description: This alert looks for an Active Directory certificate being modified with an Enrollment Agent value that allows an Active Directory principal to enroll a certificate on behalf of another user. Threat actors may abuse this misconfiguration to forge certificates and Kerberos Ticket Granting Tickets (TGT). Specifically, this misconfiguration can exploit the "ESC3" attack path via https://posts.specterops.io/certified-pre-owned-d95910965cd2. If this event is deemed suspicious, look for authentication events (4624) that utilize this certificate template. Also ensure that this change was expected and approved within the environment.
MATCH-S00902.normalized_signal_name: Suspicious Active Directory Certificate Modification - Enrollment Agent by {{user_username}}
MATCH-S00902.normalized_summary: Suspicious Active Directory Certificate Modification - Enrollment Agent by {{user_username}}
MATCH-S00902.summary_expression: Suspicious Active Directory Certificate Modification - Enrollment Agent by {{user_username}}

MATCH-S00905.description_expression: This signal looks for programmatic (PowerShell, Golang, Python or Curl) access to the Okta “users” API endpoint. This endpoint provides functionality to perform various actions on Okta user accounts such as password resets and account unlocks. A full list of functionality for this endpoint can be found in the Okta documentation here: https://developer.okta.com/docs/reference/api/users/- The “Success” field will indicate whether this API request was successful or not and the “Description” field will contain the event that was generated by the API request. Both failed and successful requests should be investigated. Ensure that this request was performed for legitimate purposes such as developer workflows or other automation mechanisms. Consider adding a match list exclusion with authorized accounts who perform requests to this Okta API endpoint via programmatic methods if this signal is triggering false positives.
MATCH-S00905.name: Okta - Programmatic Access to Users API Endpoint
MATCH-S00905.name_expression: Okta - Programmatic Access to Users API Endpoint by {{user_username}}

A full list of functionality for this endpoint can be found in the Okta documentation here: https://developer.okta.com/docs/reference/api/users/- The “Success” field will indicate whether this API request was successful or not and the “Description” field will contain the event that was generated by the API request. Both failed and successful requests should be investigated. Ensure that this request was performed for legitimate purposes such as developer workflows or other automation mechanisms. Consider adding a match list exclusion with authorized accounts who perform requests to this Okta API endpoint via programmatic methods if this signal is triggering false positives.

MATCH-S00906.description_expression: This rule looks for an Okta application being created. Ensure that this activity is expected and authorized. Only Okta administrators should be creating applications. Check the Okta administrator portal for more details regarding the application in question such as scopes and access levels. The field "fields["target.1.alternateId"]" contains the name of the application that was created
MATCH-S00906.expression:
metadata_vendor = 'Okta'
and metadata_deviceEventId = "application.lifecycle.create"
and success = true
MATCH-S00906.name: Okta - Application Created
MATCH-S00906.normalized_description: This rule looks for an Okta application being created. Ensure that this activity is expected and authorized. Only Okta administrators should be creating applications. Check the Okta administrator portal for more details regarding the application in question such as scopes and access levels. The field "fields["target.1.alternateId"]" contains the name of the application that was created
MATCH-S00906.normalized_summary: An Okta application has been created by {{user_username}} from the IP address of {{device_ip}}
MATCH-S00906.summary_expression: An Okta application has been created by {{user_username}} from the IP address of {{device_ip}}

MATCH-S00907.description_expression: An Okta policy rule has been added through the Okta admin application. The field "fields["target.1.displayName"]" contains the name of this rule for investigation purposes. Ensure that this activity is planned and performed by an authorized Okta administrator. Examine other generated signals for the user to further investigation activities.
MATCH-S00907.name: Okta - Policy Rule Added
MATCH-S00907.normalized_description: An Okta policy rule has been added through the Okta admin application. The field "fields["target.1.displayName"]" contains the name of this rule for investigation purposes. Ensure that this activity is planned and performed by an authorized Okta administrator. Examine other generated signals for the user to further investigation activities.
MATCH-S00907.normalized_summary: Okta Policy Rule Added by {{user_username}} from {{srcDevice_ip}}
MATCH-S00907.summary_expression: Okta Policy Rule Added by {{user_username}} from {{srcDevice_ip}}

MATCH-S00908.description_expression: This signal will trigger when a user denies an MFA request within the Okta authenticator application. Examine other authentication attempts for this particular user and undertake confirmation efforts to ensure that  this activity is expected and valid.
MATCH-S00908.name: Okta - MFA Request Denied by User
MATCH-S00908.normalized_description: This signal will trigger when a user denies an MFA request within the Okta authenticator application. Examine other authentication attempts for this particular user and undertake confirmation efforts to ensure that  this activity is expected and valid.
MATCH-S00908.normalized_summary: An Okta MFA Request/Prompt was Denied by {{user_username}}
MATCH-S00908.summary_expression: An Okta MFA Request/Prompt was Denied by {{user_username}}

MATCH-S00916.description_expression: Within macOS and BSD, the pwpolicy command line tool can be used for Password Policy Discovery. This detects usage of pwpolicy and getaccountpolicies for the possible enumeration of password policies in a local directory. Tuning recommendations: this rule will detect MDM and Vulnerability Scanners using pwpolicy, it is recommended to create a tuning expression to filter the baseImages of validated automated processes, such as JamF, from this detection. Understanding the context of this command usage is important in determining the intent of execution, identifying the process executing the command (i.e. baseImage) will help determine if this is an automated process (MDM or Vulnerability Scanner) or if this is a user performing discovery.
MATCH-S00916.name: macOS - pwpolicy getaccountpolicies usage detected
MATCH-S00916.name_expression: macOS - pwpolicy getaccountpolicies usage detected on {{device_hostname}}
MATCH-S00916.normalized_description: Within macOS and BSD, the pwpolicy command line tool can be used for Password Policy Discovery. This detects usage of pwpolicy and getaccountpolicies for the possible enumeration of password policies in a local directory. Tuning recommendations: this rule will detect MDM and Vulnerability Scanners using pwpolicy, it is recommended to create a tuning expression to filter the baseImages of validated automated processes, such as JamF, from this detection. Understanding the context of this command usage is important in determining the intent of execution, identifying the process executing the command (i.e. baseImage) will help determine if this is an automated process (MDM or Vulnerability Scanner) or if this is a user performing discovery.
MATCH-S00916.normalized_signal_name: macOS - pwpolicy getaccountpolicies usage detected on {{device_hostname}}

MATCH-S00917.description_expression: This PowerShell COM method allows for discovery of running application windows, along with the process path and window location coordinates.  Investigation of the host is recommended to identify the behavior leading to and around the execution of this PowerShell process.
MATCH-S00917.name: Suspicious PowerShell Application Window Discovery COM method
MATCH-S00917.name_expression: Suspicious PowerShell Application Window Discovery via COM method on {{device_hostname}}
MATCH-S00917.normalized_description: This PowerShell COM method allows for discovery of running application windows, along with the process path and window location coordinates.  Investigation of the host is recommended to identify the behavior leading to and around the execution of this PowerShell process.
MATCH-S00917.normalized_signal_name: Suspicious PowerShell Application Window Discovery via COM method on {{device_hostname}}
MATCH-S00917.normalized_summary: Suspicious use of Windows COM method via PowerShell  for Application Windows Discovery on {{device_hostname}}
MATCH-S00917.summary_expression: Suspicious use of Windows COM method via PowerShell  for Application Windows Discovery on {{device_hostname}}

MATCH-S00918.description_expression: The Pluggable Authentication Module (PAM) in Linux allows system administrators to choose how applications authenticate users. The common-password file defines behavior of password use in Linux subsystems. This detection looks for use of cat to display the contents of the common-password file, which should not be a common occurrence on systems. It is recommended to investigate the host upon which this detection occurs to understand the exposure of the password policies for the system.
MATCH-S00918.name: Suspicious cat of PAM common-password policy
MATCH-S00918.name_expression: Suspicious cat of PAM common-password policy on {{device_hostname}}
MATCH-S00918.normalized_description: The Pluggable Authentication Module (PAM) in Linux allows system administrators to choose how applications authenticate users. The common-password file defines behavior of password use in Linux subsystems. This detection looks for use of cat to display the contents of the common-password file, which should not be a common occurrence on systems. It is recommended to investigate the host upon which this detection occurs to understand the exposure of the password policies for the system.
MATCH-S00918.normalized_signal_name: Suspicious cat of PAM common-password policy on {{device_hostname}}
MATCH-S00918.normalized_summary: User: {{user_username}} ran cat on pam.d common-password on {{device_hostname}}
MATCH-S00918.summary_expression: User: {{user_username}} ran cat on pam.d common-password on {{device_hostname}}

MATCH-S00919.description_expression: The chage command on Linux allows for the changing of user password expiry information. The chage command is restricted to the root user; however, non-root/unprivileged users may use the -l flag to determine when the user’s password or account is due to expire.  It is recommended to investigate the system and account the command has been executed on, to assess the intent of this execution. Additionally, looking at the command line and parent process is helpful in identifying valid automated processes executing this command that would benefit from tuning out via Rule Tuning.
MATCH-S00919.name: chage command use on host
MATCH-S00919.name_expression: chage command usage detected on Host:{{device_hostname}}
MATCH-S00919.normalized_description: The chage command on Linux allows for the changing of user password expiry information. The chage command is restricted to the root user; however, non-root/unprivileged users may use the -l flag to determine when the user’s password or account is due to expire.  It is recommended to investigate the system and account the command has been executed on, to assess the intent of this execution. Additionally, looking at the command line and parent process is helpful in identifying valid automated processes executing this command that would benefit from tuning out via Rule Tuning.
MATCH-S00919.normalized_signal_name: chage command usage detected on Host:{{device_hostname}}
MATCH-S00919.normalized_summary: Cxecution of the chage command has been detected on Host: {{device_hostname}} by User: {{user_username}}
MATCH-S00919.summary_expression: Cxecution of the chage command has been detected on Host: {{device_hostname}} by User: {{user_username}}

MATCH-S00920.description_expression: Detects the use of PowerShell for Applicaiton Window Discovery to identify open application windows to gather information on running programs, collect potential data, and discover security tooling. Investigation into the host and user to identify the process executing the PowerShell function. Reference: https://www.ired.team/offensive-security/enumeration-and-discovery/t1010-application-window-discovery
MATCH-S00920.expression:
objectClassification = 'Endpoint'
AND (lower(commandLine) LIKE '%get-process%' OR lower(commandLine) RLIKE '\bgps\b') 
AND lower(commandLine) LIKE '%mainwindowtitle%mainwindowtitle%'
MATCH-S00920.name: Suspicious PowerShell Window Discovery Cmdlet execution
MATCH-S00920.name_expression: Suspicious PowerShell Window Discovery Cmdlet execution on {{device_hostname}}
MATCH-S00920.normalized_description: Detects the use of PowerShell for Applicaiton Window Discovery to identify open application windows to gather information on running programs, collect potential data, and discover security tooling. Investigation into the host and user to identify the process executing the PowerShell function. Reference: https://www.ired.team/offensive-security/enumeration-and-discovery/t1010-application-window-discovery
MATCH-S00920.normalized_signal_name: Suspicious PowerShell Window Discovery Cmdlet execution on {{device_hostname}}
MATCH-S00920.normalized_summary: PowerShell Window Discovery Cmdlet execution to obtain open applications using mainWindowTitle on {{device_hostname}}
MATCH-S00920.summary_expression: PowerShell Window Discovery Cmdlet execution to obtain open applications using mainWindowTitle on {{device_hostname}}

MATCH-S00921.description_expression: An AWS Bedrock Model invocation logging configuration change was observed. Ensure that this activity is expected and authorized. Take a look at the full event details, particularly the requestParameters.loggingConfig* fields in order to see what specific configuration values were changed. Telemetry and logging configuration changes should be a relatively rare occurrence in the environment.
MATCH-S00921.expression:
metadata_vendor = "Amazon AWS"
AND metadata_product = "CloudTrail"
AND application = "bedrock.amazonaws.com"
AND action = "PutModelInvocationLoggingConfiguration"
MATCH-S00921.name: AWS Bedrock Model Invocation Logging Configuration Change Observed
MATCH-S00921.name_expression: AWS Bedrock Model Invocation Logging Configuration Change Observed by {{user_username}}
MATCH-S00921.normalized_description: An AWS Bedrock Model invocation logging configuration change was observed. Ensure that this activity is expected and authorized. Take a look at the full event details, particularly the requestParameters.loggingConfig* fields in order to see what specific configuration values were changed. Telemetry and logging configuration changes should be a relatively rare occurrence in the environment.
MATCH-S00921.normalized_signal_name: AWS Bedrock Model Invocation Logging Configuration Change Observed by {{user_username}}
MATCH-S00921.normalized_summary: AWS Bedrock Model Invocation Logging Configuration Change Observed by {{user_username}} from IP {{device_ip}}
MATCH-S00921.summary_expression: AWS Bedrock Model Invocation Logging Configuration Change Observed by {{user_username}} from IP {{device_ip}}

MATCH-S00922.description_expression: This rule detects when an AWS Bedrock Agent has been created in the environment. Bedrock Agents can be configured with various parameters to build AI applications. Take a look at the "responseElements.agent.agentName" field to see the name of the agent being created. Ensure that the user creating the agent is authorized to develop AI applications within the environment.
MATCH-S00922.expression:
cloud_provider = "AWS"
AND metadata_vendor = "Amazon AWS"
AND metadata_product = "CloudTrail"
AND application = "bedrock.amazonaws.com"
AND action = "CreateAgent"
MATCH-S00922.name: AWS Bedrock Agent Created
MATCH-S00922.normalized_description: This rule detects when an AWS Bedrock Agent has been created in the environment. Bedrock Agents can be configured with various parameters to build AI applications. Take a look at the "responseElements.agent.agentName" field to see the name of the agent being created. Ensure that the user creating the agent is authorized to develop AI applications within the environment.
MATCH-S00922.normalized_summary: AWS Bedrock Agent Created by {{user_username}} with the role {{user_username_role}}
MATCH-S00922.summary_expression: AWS Bedrock Agent Created by {{user_username}} with the role {{user_username_role}}

MATCH-S00923.description_expression: A user has attempted to invoke a model via AWS Bedrock for which access was denied due to a permission issue. This event can be a normal occurrence for a user who has not been provisioned the proper IAM resources for AWS Bedrock. However, it could also be a malicious attempt at running a particular model via AWS Bedrock. Take a look at the username, IP address, role type, role and model via the fields["requestParameters.modelId"] field.
MATCH-S00923.expression:
metadata_vendor = "Amazon AWS"
AND metadata_product = "CloudTrail"
AND application = "bedrock.amazonaws.com"
AND action = "InvokeModel"
AND fields["errorCode"] = "AccessDenied"
MATCH-S00923.name: AWS Bedrock Model Invocation Denied for User
MATCH-S00923.name_expression: AWS Bedrock Model Invocation Denied for {{user_username}}
MATCH-S00923.normalized_description: A user has attempted to invoke a model via AWS Bedrock for which access was denied due to a permission issue. This event can be a normal occurrence for a user who has not been provisioned the proper IAM resources for AWS Bedrock. However, it could also be a malicious attempt at running a particular model via AWS Bedrock. Take a look at the username, IP address, role type, role and model via the fields["requestParameters.modelId"] field.
MATCH-S00923.normalized_signal_name: AWS Bedrock Model Invocation Denied for {{user_username}}
MATCH-S00923.normalized_summary: AWS Bedrock Model Invocation Denied for {{user_username}}
MATCH-S00923.summary_expression: AWS Bedrock Model Invocation Denied for {{user_username}}

MATCH-S00924.description_expression: AWS Bedrock Guardrails provide users with the ability to configure options like filtering out harmful content or defining denied topics for models. Guardrails also allow the blocking of sensitive information such as PII. Ensure that this deletion was performed by an authorized user during an expected change. Look at other activity from this user account, focusing on the Bedrock service and pivoting from there if the event is deemed suspicious.
MATCH-S00924.expression:
metadata_vendor = "Amazon AWS"
AND metadata_product = "CloudTrail"
AND application = "bedrock.amazonaws.com"
AND action = "DeleteGuardrail"
MATCH-S00924.name: AWS Bedrock Guardrail Deleted
MATCH-S00924.normalized_description: AWS Bedrock Guardrails provide users with the ability to configure options like filtering out harmful content or defining denied topics for models. Guardrails also allow the blocking of sensitive information such as PII. Ensure that this deletion was performed by an authorized user during an expected change. Look at other activity from this user account, focusing on the Bedrock service and pivoting from there if the event is deemed suspicious.

MATCH-S00925.description_expression: Trufflehog is a tool that can be utilized to find and verify secrets. When Trufflehog locates AWS credentials, it attempts to validate them using the GetCallerIdentity API call. This signal looks for the default Trufflehog User Agent within CloudTrail telemetry, combined with the GetCallerIdentity API call. Within this telemetry, the user_username field will contain the value of the username associated with the secret or credential that Trufflehog is attempting to verify. Look at the events surrounding the source IP address of this event. Look for any potential areas that may have contained keys or secrets for the user_username value.
MATCH-S00925.expression:
metadata_vendor = "Amazon AWS"
and metadata_product = "CloudTrail"
and lower(action) = "getcalleridentity"
and lower(http_userAgent) = "trufflehog"
MATCH-S00925.name: Trufflehog AWS Credential Verification Detected
MATCH-S00925.normalized_description: Trufflehog is a tool that can be utilized to find and verify secrets. When Trufflehog locates AWS credentials, it attempts to validate them using the GetCallerIdentity API call. This signal looks for the default Trufflehog User Agent within CloudTrail telemetry, combined with the GetCallerIdentity API call. Within this telemetry, the user_username field will contain the value of the username associated with the secret or credential that Trufflehog is attempting to verify. Look at the events surrounding the source IP address of this event. Look for any potential areas that may have contained keys or secrets for the user_username value.
MATCH-S00925.normalized_summary: Trufflehog AWS Credential Verification Detected for {{user_username}}  from IP: {{srcDevice_ip}}
MATCH-S00925.summary_expression: Trufflehog AWS Credential Verification Detected for {{user_username}}  from IP: {{srcDevice_ip}}

MATCH-S00949.description_expression: Detects vulnerability alerts created for a GitHub repository.
MATCH-S00949.expression:
metadata_product = 'GitHub Enterprise Audit'
AND action in ('repository_vulnerability_alert.auto_reopen','repository_vulnerability_alert.create','repository_vulnerability_alert.reintroduce','repository_vulnerability_alert.reopen')
MATCH-S00949.name: GitHub - Vulnerability Alerts
MATCH-S00949.normalized_description: Detects vulnerability alerts created for a GitHub repository.
MATCH-S00949.normalized_summary: GitHub Security Advisory ID: {{fields['ghsa_id']}} detected on {{repository}} with Alert ID: {{fields['alert_number']}}
MATCH-S00949.summary_expression: GitHub Security Advisory ID: {{fields['ghsa_id']}} detected on {{repository}} with Alert ID: {{fields['alert_number']}}

MATCH-S00950.commit_message: Added logging requirements of GitHub Enterprise to the description of GitHub rules released 6 Dec 2024
MATCH-S00950.description_expression: Detects new user additions or invitations to the business or organizations GitHub. New user additions/invitations should be monitored as they could be a vector for malicious actors to establish access or persistence. This detection was developed utilizing GitHub Enterprise Audit logging. Refer to https://help.sumologic.com/docs/integrations/app-development/github/#collecting-logs-for-github for instructions on ingesting GitHub Enterprise Audit logs.
MATCH-S00950.expression:
metadata_product = 'GitHub Enterprise Audit'
AND action in ('org.invite_member','org.add_member')
MATCH-S00950.name: GitHub - Member Invitation or Addition
MATCH-S00950.name_expression: GitHub - User {{targetUser_username}} was Added or Invited to the {{fields['org']}}
MATCH-S00950.normalized_description: Detects new user additions or invitations to the business or organizations GitHub. New user additions/invitations should be monitored as they could be a vector for malicious actors to establish access or persistence. This detection was developed utilizing GitHub Enterprise Audit logging. Refer to https://help.sumologic.com/docs/integrations/app-development/github/#collecting-logs-for-github for instructions on ingesting GitHub Enterprise Audit logs.
MATCH-S00950.normalized_signal_name: GitHub - User {{targetUser_username}} was Added or Invited to the {{fields['org']}}
MATCH-S00950.normalized_summary: {{user_username}}  invited or added {{targetUser_username}}  to the {{fields['org']}}  organization within the {{fields['business']}}  business
MATCH-S00950.summary_expression: {{user_username}}  invited or added {{targetUser_username}}  to the {{fields['org']}}  organization within the {{fields['business']}}  business

MATCH-S00951.commit_message: Added logging requirements of GitHub Enterprise to the description of GitHub rules released 6 Dec 2024
MATCH-S00951.description_expression: Observes for secret scanning alerts from GitHub. Secrets detected by GitHub Enterprise Cloud undergo validation by GitHub automatically, to determine whether they are actively in use, this is not surfaced in the audit log, and will require separate inspection. For more information see : [Evaluating alerts from secret scanning](https://docs.github.com/en/code-security/secret-scanning/managing-alerts-from-secret-scanning/evaluating-alerts). This detection was developed utilizing GitHub Enterprise Audit logging. Refer to https://help.sumologic.com/docs/integrations/app-development/github/#collecting-logs-for-github for instructions on ingesting GitHub Enterprise Audit logs.
MATCH-S00951.expression:
metadata_product = 'GitHub Enterprise Audit'
AND action in ('secret_scanning_alert.create','secret_scanning_alert.reopen')
MATCH-S00951.name: GitHub - Secret Scanning Alert
MATCH-S00951.normalized_description: Observes for secret scanning alerts from GitHub. Secrets detected by GitHub Enterprise Cloud undergo validation by GitHub automatically, to determine whether they are actively in use, this is not surfaced in the audit log, and will require separate inspection. For more information see : [Evaluating alerts from secret scanning](https://docs.github.com/en/code-security/secret-scanning/managing-alerts-from-secret-scanning/evaluating-alerts). This detection was developed utilizing GitHub Enterprise Audit logging. Refer to https://help.sumologic.com/docs/integrations/app-development/github/#collecting-logs-for-github for instructions on ingesting GitHub Enterprise Audit logs.
MATCH-S00951.normalized_summary: GitHub has generated secret scanning alert #{{fields['number']}} for the {{fields['repository']}} within the {{fields['org']}} organization
MATCH-S00951.summary_expression: GitHub has generated secret scanning alert #{{fields['number']}} for the {{fields['repository']}} within the {{fields['org']}} organization

Illegitimate addition of administrative users could be an indication of priviledge escalation or persistence by adversaries. This detection was developed utilizing GitHub Enterprise Audit logging. Refer to https://help.sumologic.com/docs/integrations/app-development/github/#collecting-logs-for-github for instructions on ingesting GitHub Enterprise Audit logs.
MATCH-S00952.expression:
metadata_product = 'GitHub Enterprise Audit'
AND action in ('business.invite_admin','business.add_admin')

MATCH-S00952.name_expression: GitHub - {{targetUser_username}} Added or Invited as a GitHub Administrator

MATCH-S00953.commit_message: Added logging requirements of GitHub Enterprise to the description of GitHub rules released 6 Dec 2024
MATCH-S00953.description_expression: Detects modifications to the GitHub Enterprise Audit Log. Modifications and deletions have the potential to reduce visibility of malicious activity. This detection was developed utilizing GitHub Enterprise Audit logging. Refer to https://help.sumologic.com/docs/integrations/app-development/github/#collecting-logs-for-github for instructions on ingesting GitHub Enterprise Audit logs.
MATCH-S00953.expression:
metadata_product = 'GitHub Enterprise Audit'
AND action IN ('audit_log_streaming.destroy','audit_log_streaming.update')

MATCH-S00956.commit_message: Added logging requirements of GitHub Enterprise to the description of GitHub rules released 6 Dec 2024
MATCH-S00956.description_expression: Detects OAuth application activities within GitHub. OAuth application management and access activity should be monitored for potential abuse by malicous actors, either by creating malicious access paths within GitHub, or destruction of GitHub infrastructure. This detection was developed utilizing GitHub Enterprise Audit logging. Refer to https://help.sumologic.com/docs/integrations/app-development/github/#collecting-logs-for-github for instructions on ingesting GitHub Enterprise Audit logs.
MATCH-S00956.expression:
metadata_product = 'GitHub Enterprise Audit'
AND action in ('oauth_application.create','oauth_application.destroy','oauth_application.generate_client_secret','oauth_application.remove_client_secret','oauth_application.reset_secret','oauth_application.revoke_all_tokens','oauth_application.revoke_tokens','oauth_application.transfer','org.disable_oauth_app_restrictions','org.enable_oauth_app_restrictions','org.oauth_app_access_approved','org.oauth_app_access_denied','org.oauth_app_access_requested')
MATCH-S00956.name: GitHub - OAuth Application Activity
MATCH-S00956.normalized_description: Detects OAuth application activities within GitHub. OAuth application management and access activity should be monitored for potential abuse by malicous actors, either by creating malicious access paths within GitHub, or destruction of GitHub infrastructure. This detection was developed utilizing GitHub Enterprise Audit logging. Refer to https://help.sumologic.com/docs/integrations/app-development/github/#collecting-logs-for-github for instructions on ingesting GitHub Enterprise Audit logs.
MATCH-S00956.normalized_summary: OAuth operation: {{action}} performed by {{user_username}}within the {{fields['org']}} organization
MATCH-S00956.summary_expression: OAuth operation: {{action}} performed by {{user_username}}within the {{fields['org']}} organization

MATCH-S00993.description_expression:
Context:
AzureDevOps retains items such as run records and produced artifacts for a configurable amount of time. An attacker can cover up evidence of malicious activity by reducing the retention time.

Detection:
This detection monitors for any reduction in the pipeline retention settings.

Tuning Recommendations:
n/a

Recommended Actions:
Determine whether the retention setting reduction was an approved change (for example, look for a change control ticket).
Investigate the user who reduced the retention setting for indications of account compromise or insider threat activity.

Credits:
This detection is based on the Azure Sentinel ADORetentionReduced.yaml rule.
MATCH-S00993.expression:
metadata_vendor = "Microsoft"
AND metadata_product = "Azure DevOps Auditing"
AND metadata_deviceEventId = "AzureDevOpsAuditEvent"
AND action = "Pipelines.PipelineRetentionSettingChanged"
AND fields['data.Data.OldValue'] > fields['data.Data.NewValue']
MATCH-S00993.name: Azure DevOps - Pipeline Retention Settings Reduced
MATCH-S00993.name_expression: Azure DevOps - Pipeline Retention Settings Reduced by User: {{user_username}}
MATCH-S00993.normalized_description:

Recommended Actions:

Investigate the user who reduced the retention setting for indications of account compromise or insider threat activity.

Credits:
This detection is based on the Azure Sentinel ADORetentionReduced.yaml rule.
MATCH-S00993.normalized_signal_name: Azure DevOps - Pipeline Retention Settings Reduced by User: {{user_username}}
MATCH-S00993.normalized_summary: User: {{user_username}}  has reduced the Pipeline Retention settings
MATCH-S00993.summary_expression: User: {{user_username}}  has reduced the Pipeline Retention settings

MATCH-S00994.description_expression:
Context:
Any changes to administrative groups such as Project Administrators or Project Collection Administrators is noteworthy as they result in the user having a broad set of permissions.

Detection:
This detection monitors for changes to the following groups: Project Administrators, Project Collection Administrators, Project Collection Service Accounts, Build Administrator

Recommended Actions:
Determine whether the group modification was an approved change (for example, look for a change control ticket).
Investigate the user who modified the group for indications of account compromise or other insider threat activity.

Tuning Recommendations:
n/a

Credits:
This detection is based on the Azure Sentinel NewPAPCAPCASaddedtoADO.yaml rule.
MATCH-S00994.expression:
metadata_vendor = "Microsoft"
AND metadata_product = "Azure DevOps Auditing"
AND metadata_deviceEventId = "AzureDevOpsAuditEvent"
AND changeTarget RLIKE "(Project Administrators|Project Collection Administrators|Project Collection Service Accounts|Build Administrator)"

MATCH-S00996.description_expression:
Context:
Authentication to Azure DevOps via a Personal Access Token (PAT) is intended for particular use cases and should not be observed with all actions in Azure. This Alert detects whenever a PAT is used in ways that PATs are not normally used. If unusual PAT use is observed it potentially indicates the PAT was stolen by an attacker.

Detection:
This detection monitors for use of a Personal Access Token in conjunction with categories of action that aren’t normally associated with PAT authentication.

Recommended Actions:
Determine whether the PAT use is part of a known and approved use case.
Investigate the user whose PAT was used for indications of account compromise or other insider threat activity. 
Investigate the workstation of the user for indications of compromise.

Tuning Recommendations:
If your organization has known use cases, such as automation, that trigger this rule, add a tuning expression to exclude either the action or other distinguishing metadata such as the user.

Credits:
This detection is based on the Azure Sentinel AzDOPatSessionMisuse.yaml rule.
MATCH-S00996.expression:
metadata_vendor = "Microsoft"
AND metadata_product = "Azure DevOps Auditing"
AND metadata_deviceEventId = "AzureDevOpsAuditEvent"
AND lower(fields['data.AuthenticationMechanism']) LIKE "pat_%"
AND 
    ( 
        action RLIKE '^(Security|Project|AuditLog|Extension)'
        OR 
        action RLIKE '(Group\.UpdateGroupMembership\.Add|Library\.ServiceConnectionExecuted|Pipelines\.PipelineModified|Release\.ReleasePipelineModified|Git\.RefUpdatePoliciesBypassed)'
    )
MATCH-S00996.name: Azure DevOps - Personal Access Token (PAT) Misuse Observed
MATCH-S00996.name_expression: Azure DevOps - Personal Access Token (PAT) Misuse Observed
MATCH-S00996.normalized_description:

Credits:

MATCH-S00996.normalized_summary: User: {{user_username}} Personal Access Token has been used suspiciously
MATCH-S00996.summary_expression: User: {{user_username}} Personal Access Token has been used suspiciously

MATCH-S00997.description_expression:
Context:
Personal Access Tokens (PATs) can be used to authenticate into Azure DevOps. PATs are intended to be used for authenticating automated access to Azure rather than for interactive use cases. Observing PAT authentication in interactive use cases could indicate that the PAT was stolen.

Detection:
This detection monitors for the use of a PAT for authentication from a User Agent String indicating a web browser.

Recommended Actions:
Investigate the user account associated with the Personal Access Token and the user’s workstation for signs of compromise.

Tuning Recommendations:
If your organization has known legitimate use cases for PAT authentication with a browser, add a tuning expression specific to the known use case. For example, you may tune out specific users, user agent strings or IP addresses as known use cases.

Credits:
This detection is based on the Azure Sentinel ADOPATUsedWithBrowser.yaml rule.
MATCH-S00997.expression:
metadata_vendor = "Microsoft"
AND metadata_product = "Azure DevOps Auditing"
AND metadata_deviceEventId = "AzureDevOpsAuditEvent"
AND lower(fields['data.AuthenticationMechanism']) LIKE "pat_%"
AND lower(http_userAgent) RLIKE "(gecko|webkit|presto|trident|edgehtml|blink)"
MATCH-S00997.name: Azure DevOps - Browser Observed in Personal Access Token (PAT) Use
MATCH-S00997.name_expression: Azure DevOps - Browser Observed in Personal Access Token (PAT) Use by {{user_username}}
MATCH-S00997.normalized_description:

This detection monitors for the use of a PAT for authentication from a User Agent String indicating a web browser.

Recommended Actions:

Tuning Recommendations:
If your organization has known legitimate use cases for PAT authentication with a browser, add a tuning expression specific to the known use case. For example, you may tune out specific users, user agent strings or IP addresses as known use cases.

Credits:
This detection is based on the Azure Sentinel ADOPATUsedWithBrowser.yaml rule.

MATCH-S00997.normalized_summary: User: {{user_username}} has been observed using a PAT with a browser
MATCH-S00997.summary_expression: User: {{user_username}} has been observed using a PAT with a browser

MATCH-S00998.description_expression:
Context:
The IBM X-Force Red team released the Azure DevOps Services Attack Toolkit (ADOKit) for Black Hat Arsenal 2024.

Detection:
This is a simple detection matching on “ADOKit” at the start of the HTTP User Agent String (UAS). This detection effectively catches basic ADOKit use. It is brittle to attackers changing the User Agent String to another more innocuous browser to mask the traffic.

Recommended Actions: 
Determine the user using the ADOKit tooling and the activities they have taken against the Azure DevOps environment.

Tuning Recommendations:
Create a rule tuning expression to tune out accounts permitted to use ADOKit, such as red teams, penetration testers, or admin users. 

Credits:
This detection is based on the Azure Sentinel ADOMaliciousToolingDetections1.yaml detection.
MATCH-S00998.expression:
metadata_vendor = "Microsoft"
AND metadata_product = "Azure DevOps Auditing"
AND metadata_deviceEventId = "AzureDevOpsAuditEvent"
AND http_userAgent LIKE "ADOKit%"
MATCH-S00998.name: Azure DevOps - Known Malicious Tooling Detected ADOKit
MATCH-S00998.name_expression: Azure DevOps - ADOKit malicious tooling from IP: {{srcDevice_ip}}
MATCH-S00998.normalized_description:

MATCH-S01025.commit_message: TLAB-2253: add list exclusions to hasThreatMatch rules (#5864)
MATCH-S01025.description_expression:
This rule detects allowed inbound traffic from an IP address with a low confidence rating from a threat intelligence feed.
Note that this rule is disabled by default due to a high likelihood of excessive signal volume and risk assignment to entities triggering this rule as-is. Tuning to reduce signal volume and potential false positives is highly recommended before activating this rule.
MATCH-S01025.expression:
srcDevice_ip_isInternal = False
AND success = True
AND hasThreatMatch([srcDevice_ip], (confidence > 1 AND confidence < 50)
AND (type='ipv4-addr' OR type='ipv6-addr'))
AND NOT (objectType = 'Authentication' AND success = True) 
AND NOT (array_contains(listMatches, 'business_asns') OR array_contains(listMatches, 'business_domains') OR array_contains(listMatches, 'business_hostnames') OR array_contains(listMatches, 'business_ips') OR array_contains(listMatches, 'sandbox_ips') OR array_contains(listMatches, 'verified_domains') OR array_contains(listMatches, 'verified_hostnames') OR array_contains(listMatches, 'verified_ips'))
MATCH-S01025.name: Threat Intel - Inbound Traffic from Threat Feed IP (Low Confidence)
MATCH-S01025.name_expression: Threat Intel - Inbound Traffic From Low Confidence Threat IP

MATCH-S01049.commit_message: TLAB-2126 Silent Mode rules for Sharepoint CVE (#5825)
MATCH-S01049.expression:
(
baseImage like '%w3wp.exe'
OR
parentBaseImage like '%w3wp.exe'
OR
commandLine like '%w3wp.exe'
OR
parentCommandLine like '%w3wp.exe'
)
AND
(
file_path like '%\TEMPLATE\LAYOUTS\%spinstall%.aspx'
OR
file_basename like '%\TEMPLATE\LAYOUTS\%spinstall%.aspx'
OR
commandLine like '%spinstall%.aspx%'
OR
changeTarget like '%spinstall%.aspx'
)
MATCH-S01049.name: CVE-2025-53770 Detection spinstall0.aspx

MATCH-S01050.commit_message: TLAB-2165: added toolshell rules and purgatory test cases (#5832)
MATCH-S01050.description_expression:
Context:
Executable files added to Microsoft Internet Information Server (IIS) directories can indicate the installation of a web shell by an attacker. For example, the ToolShell exploit (CVE-2025-53770, CVE-2025-53771) included the installation of spinstall10.aspx in an executable directory.

Detection:
This detection will trigger when files executable by IIS are added to directories that frequently have execute permissions.

Recommended Actions:
Determine whether the addition of the executable file was an expected action, and if not, consider disconnecting the IIS server from the network for forensic analysis. Review logs indicating  unusual network activity originating from the IIS server to other internal assets.

Tuning Recommendations: 
Add executable directories specific to your IIS installation(s) to the rule expression by duplicating the rule and modifying it. Add exclusions as necessary to processes expected to add IIS script files to IIS directories.
MATCH-S01050.expression:
normalizedAction = 'create'
and normalizedResource = 'file'
AND changeTarget MATCHES /(?i:\\wwwroot\\|\\windows\\microsoft\.net\\framework\\|\\microsoft shared\\web server extensions\\).+\.(?i:as[hmp]x|cshtml)$/
AND baseImage NOT MATCHES /(?i)(?:\\w3wp|\\msdeploy|\\svchost|\\explorer)\.exe$/
MATCH-S01050.name: IIS - Executable File Added to Directory
MATCH-S01050.name_expression: IIS - Executable File Added to Directory on host {{device_hostname}}
MATCH-S01050.normalized_description:
Context:
Executable files added to Microsoft Internet Information Server (IIS) directories can indicate the installation of a web shell by an attacker. For example, the ToolShell exploit (CVE-2025-53770, CVE-2025-53771) included the installation of spinstall10.aspx in an executable directory.

Detection:
This detection will trigger when files executable by IIS are added to directories that frequently have execute permissions.

Recommended Actions:
Determine whether the addition of the executable file was an expected action, and if not, consider disconnecting the IIS server from the network for forensic analysis. Review logs indicating  unusual network activity originating from the IIS server to other internal assets.

Tuning Recommendations: 
Add executable directories specific to your IIS installation(s) to the rule expression by duplicating the rule and modifying it. Add exclusions as necessary to processes expected to add IIS script files to IIS directories.
MATCH-S01050.normalized_signal_name: IIS - Executable File Added to Directory on host {{device_hostname}}
MATCH-S01050.normalized_summary: The executable file {{changeTarget}}   was added by process {{baseImage}}
MATCH-S01050.summary_expression: The executable file {{changeTarget}}   was added by process {{baseImage}}

MATCH-S01051.commit_message: TLAB-2165: added toolshell rules and purgatory test cases (#5832)
MATCH-S01051.description_expression:
Context:
Exploits against two vulnerabilities in Microsoft SharePoint server, CVE-2025-53770 and CVE-2025-53771, are combined to execute code on Microsoft SharePoint without authentication. This attack has been nicknamed "ToolShell".

Detection:
This detection will trigger on the inital access and uploading of a malicious payload to the vulnerable SharePoint server.

Recommended Actions:
Follow the Microsoft guidance for how to protect against and remediate this attack: https://msrc.microsoft.com/blog/2025/07/customer-guidance-for-sharepoint-vulnerability-cve-2025-53770/#how-to-protect-your-environment
MATCH-S01051.expression:
http_method = 'POST'
AND http_response_statusCode IN (200, 302)
AND http_referer_path MATCHES /(?i)_layouts\/signout\.aspx$/
AND fields['cs_uri_stem'] MATCHES /(?i)_layouts\/1[56]\/toolpane\.aspx$/
MATCH-S01051.name: SharePoint Server ToolShell Exploitation (CVE-2025-53770, CVE-2025-53771)
MATCH-S01051.normalized_description:
Context:
Exploits against two vulnerabilities in Microsoft SharePoint server, CVE-2025-53770 and CVE-2025-53771, are combined to execute code on Microsoft SharePoint without authentication. This attack has been nicknamed "ToolShell".

Detection:
This detection will trigger on the inital access and uploading of a malicious payload to the vulnerable SharePoint server.

Recommended Actions:

MATCH-S01052.commit_message: TLAB-2165: added toolshell rules and purgatory test cases (#5832)
MATCH-S01052.description_expression:
Context:
Exploits against two vulnerabilities in Microsoft SharePoint server, CVE-2025-53770 and CVE-2025-53771 are combined to execute code on Microsoft SharePoint without authentication. This attack has been nicknamed "ToolShell".

Detection:
This detection will trigger on GET requests occurring to the web shell post-exploitation .

Recommended Actions:
Follow the Microsoft guidance for how to protect against and remediate this attack: https://msrc.microsoft.com/blog/2025/07/customer-guidance-for-sharepoint-vulnerability-cve-2025-53770/#how-to-protect-your-environment
MATCH-S01052.expression:
http_method = 'GET'
AND http_response_statusCode IN (200,302)
AND fields['cs_uri_stem'] MATCHES /(?i)_layouts\/1[56]\/spinstall\d{0,2}\.aspx/
MATCH-S01052.name: SharePoint Server ToolShell Web Shell Interaction (CVE-2025-53771)

MATCH-S01056.commit_message: TLAB-2265: Updates to Brute Force RDP rules (#5865)
MATCH-S01056.description_expression:
Detection:
This rule triggers on a successful remote interactive login (such as via RDP)  of a privileged .

Scenario:
An attacker who has obtained credentials for a privileged account proceeds to log in to one or more hosts over RDP to further their objectives.

Recommended Actions:
Note that this rule is more relevant in organizations with policies regarding acceptable use of administrative accounts. Determine whether the privileged login is normal activity by reviewing the source IP address and its geo-location, the time of day, whether the account’s role warrants login to the destination host and potentially contacting the user. If the login is not normal activity, log out all sessions for the compromised account, block the source IP address at the firewall, and review all activity by the account within the timeframe of the login.

Tuning Recommendations:
If appropriate, create a matchlist named RDP_admin_hosts and add names of hosts that are known and approved to have remote administrative logins.
MATCH-S01056.expression:
logonType = 'RemoteInteractive'
AND normalizedAction = 'logon'
AND objectClassification = 'Authentication'
AND user_role = 'Administrator'
AND success = true
AND NOT(array_contains(listMatches,'RDP_admin_hosts'))
MATCH-S01056.name: Administrative Remote Interactive Login
MATCH-S01056.normalized_description:
Detection:
This rule triggers on a successful remote interactive login (such as via RDP)  of a privileged .

Scenario:

Recommended Actions:
Note that this rule is more relevant in organizations with policies regarding acceptable use of administrative accounts. Determine whether the privileged login is normal activity by reviewing the source IP address and its geo-location, the time of day, whether the account’s role warrants login to the destination host and potentially contacting the user. If the login is not normal activity, log out all sessions for the compromised account, block the source IP address at the firewall, and review all activity by the account within the timeframe of the login.

Tuning Recommendations:
If appropriate, create a matchlist named RDP_admin_hosts and add names of hosts that are known and approved to have remote administrative logins.
MATCH-S01056.normalized_summary: Successful administrative RDP login from IP: {{srcDevice_ip}} for user: {{user_username}}
MATCH-S01056.summary_expression: Successful administrative RDP login from IP: {{srcDevice_ip}} for user: {{user_username}}

OUTLIER-S00001.description_expression: Detects excessive failed login attempts for the same username based on a daily outlier standard deviation for said user. This is designed to catch both slow and quick brute force type attacks using a user specific historic baseline. The minimum floor of failures expected by default is set to 10.
OUTLIER-S00001.match_expression:
objectType = 'Authentication'
AND normalizedAction = 'logon'
AND success = false
AND NOT
    (metadata_deviceEventId = 'Security-4776'
    AND (array_contains(listMatches, 'domain_controllers')))
AND NOT
    (metadata_vendor = 'Microsoft'
    AND metadata_product = 'Azure'
    AND fields['resultType'] = '700082')
AND NOT
    (metadata_vendor IN ('Microsoft','Intersect Alliance')
    AND metadata_product IN ('Windows','Snare Enterprise Agent for Windows')
    AND user_username like '%$%')
AND NOT array_contains(listMatches, 'vuln_scanners')
OUTLIER-S00001.name: Spike in Login Failures from a User
OUTLIER-S00001.name_expression: Spike in Login Failures from a User
OUTLIER-S00001.normalized_description: Detects excessive failed login attempts for the same username based on a daily outlier standard deviation for said user. This is designed to catch both slow and quick brute force type attacks using a user specific historic baseline. The minimum floor of failures expected by default is set to 10.
OUTLIER-S00001.normalized_signal_name: Spike in Login Failures from a User
OUTLIER-S00001.normalized_summary: Excessive count of failure login events identified for user: {{user_username}} based on daily historic activity
OUTLIER-S00001.summary_expression: Excessive count of failure login events identified for user: {{user_username}} based on daily historic activity

OUTLIER-S00002.commit_message: TLAB-766 Move Outlier Rules out of Prototype
OUTLIER-S00002.description_expression: When attempting to pivot within an internal AD network, attacks will attempt to discover and traverse the available network shares. This detects successful and unique share access attempts for the same username based on a daily outlier standard deviation using a designated historic baseline. The minimum floor of unique shares expected by default is set to 5.
OUTLIER-S00002.match_expression:
metadata_vendor = 'Microsoft'
AND metadata_product = 'Windows'
AND metadata_deviceEventId = 'Security-5140'
AND NOT srcDevice_ip = '127.0.0.1'
AND NOT resource like '%Printer%'
AND fields["Keywords"] = "Audit Success"
AND resource != "\\*\SYSVOL"
AND resource != "\\*\IPC$"
OUTLIER-S00002.name: Spike in Successful Distinct Share Access

OUTLIER-S00004.commit_message: TLAB-766 Move Outlier Rules out of Prototype
OUTLIER-S00004.description_expression: This rule is designed to detect spikes of excessive firewall deny events within Azure for the same source IP based on a daily outliers standard deviation using a designated historic baseline. The minimum floor of firewall denies expected by default is set to 30. Customers will need to adjust the Model Sensitivity threshold (standard deviation multiplier) and Minimum Count Value of this rule to align with their environment's normal vs abnormal firewall traffic patterns.
OUTLIER-S00004.match_expression:
metadata_product = 'Azure'
AND description = 'AzureFirewallNetworkRuleLog'
OR description = 'AzureFirewallNetworkRuleLog'
AND normalizedAction = 'deny'

OUTLIER-S00005.description_expression: This rule is designed to detect spikes of excessive API call events within AWS performed by a user based on a daily outliers standard deviation using a designated historic baseline. The minimum floor of API Calls expected by default is set to 5. If excessive signaling is observed it is recommended adding expected users that are known to be involved with specific administrative or privileged activity in AWS to the AWS_admin_users match list.
OUTLIER-S00005.match_expression:
metadata_vendor = 'Amazon AWS'
AND metadata_product = 'CloudTrail'
AND user_username_role != "cloudhealthreadonly"
AND NOT isEmpty(user_username)
AND fields["userIdentity.type"] = "IAMUser" AND NOT array_contains(listMatches, 'AWS_admin_users')

The minumum floor of unique systems expected by default is set to 1.
OUTLIER-S00007.match_expression:
metadata_vendor = 'Microsoft'
AND metadata_product = 'Windows'
AND metadata_deviceEventId = 'Security-4672'
AND NOT user_username = 'system'
AND NOT user_username RLIKE '(\$$)'
AND NOT user_username RLIKE '(dwm\-)'
AND NOT user_username RLIKE '(local service|network service)'
AND NOT user_username RLIKE '(iusr)'AND NOT (
    LOWER(user_username) LIKE '%svc%'
)

This type of anomaly can linked with web based execution of Command and Control. The minimum URL length expected by default is set to 128 characters.

))
 AND NOT array_contains(listMatches, 'http_servers')

OUTLIER-S00013.description_expression: A larger than typical amount of data has been observed being sent outbound from a Sensitive Device or an Admin user. It is recommended to investigate the device associated with this IP, the Internet destinations and traffic associated with this anomalous behavior. A normalized record search for the source IP and external network traffic, within the period of time of the detection will help identify suspicious activity. This rule is dependent on the Match Lists "domain_controllers" and "admin_usernames" being populated with the sensitive device IPs and admin usernames. Additionally, Entity Tagging offers a similar and extensible alternative to Match Lists. To add more sensitive Match Lists, or Entity Tagging, please use Rule Tuning Expressions. For further customization, consider adjusting the severity of this rule and/or using entity severity as needed to increase the severity of this rule.
OUTLIER-S00013.match_expression:
objectClassification = 'Network'
AND srcDevice_ip_isInternal = true
AND dstDevice_ip_isInternal = false
AND bytesOut is not Null
AND (
    array_contains(listMatches, 'admin_username')
    OR 
    array_contains(listMatches, 'domain_controllers')
    )

OUTLIER-S00016.description_expression: This signal looks for an outlier in the number of OpenID Connect (OIDC) token request failures for an Okta client application. Use the Okta admin portal to correlate the Client ID ( mapped to user_username ) to determine what application is being targeted. Pivot off the Client ID and IP address values to examine the raw Okta events in order to ensure that this activity is planned and expected. This activity can occur during setup and development of Okta applications and integrations.
OUTLIER-S00016.match_expression:
metadata_vendor = 'Okta'
and metadata_deviceEventId = 'app.oauth2.token.grant'
and success = false

OUTLIER-S00016.summary_expression: Okta - Outlier in OIDC token request failures from {{device_ip}} for the Client ID:  {{user_username}}

OUTLIER-S00017.description_expression: This signal builds an hourly baseline of MFA denied events per user and triggers when an outlier in the number of denied attempts is detected. This signal is designed to trigger on MFA-fatigue type attacks. If false positives are detected, consider excluding certain users from the alerting logic or raise the minimum count value within the rule configuration.
OUTLIER-S00017.match_expression:
metadata_vendor = "Okta"
and description = "Authentication of user via MFA"
and fields["debugContext.debugData.pushOnlyResponseType"] = "OV_RESPONSE_DENY"
OUTLIER-S00017.name: Okta - Outlier in MFA Attempts Denied by User
OUTLIER-S00017.name_expression: Okta - Outlier in MFA Attempts Denied by {{user_username}}
OUTLIER-S00017.normalized_description: This signal builds an hourly baseline of MFA denied events per user and triggers when an outlier in the number of denied attempts is detected. This signal is designed to trigger on MFA-fatigue type attacks. If false positives are detected, consider excluding certain users from the alerting logic or raise the minimum count value within the rule configuration.
OUTLIER-S00017.normalized_signal_name: Okta - Outlier in MFA Attempts Denied by {{user_username}}
OUTLIER-S00017.normalized_summary: Outlier in Okta MFA Attempts Denied by {{user_username}} from {{srcDevice_ip}}
OUTLIER-S00017.summary_expression: Outlier in Okta MFA Attempts Denied by {{user_username}} from {{srcDevice_ip}}

OUTLIER-S00018.description_expression: This signal looks for an outlier in the number of distinct autonomous system numbers (ASNs) that a particular user utilizes to access Okta resources within an hour time period. This is designed to alert on various forms of token or credential theft as well as general Okta session anomalies.
OUTLIER-S00018.name: Okta - Outlier in ASNs Used to Access Applications
OUTLIER-S00018.name_expression: Okta - Outlier in ASNs Used to Access Applications by {{user_username}}
OUTLIER-S00018.normalized_description: This signal looks for an outlier in the number of distinct autonomous system numbers (ASNs) that a particular user utilizes to access Okta resources within an hour time period. This is designed to alert on various forms of token or credential theft as well as general Okta session anomalies.
OUTLIER-S00018.normalized_signal_name: Okta - Outlier in ASNs Used to Access Applications by {{user_username}}
OUTLIER-S00018.normalized_summary: Detected an outlier in the number of ASNs used to access Okta applications by {{user_username}}
OUTLIER-S00018.summary_expression: Detected an outlier in the number of ASNs used to access Okta applications by {{user_username}}

OUTLIER-S00019.description_expression: An outlier in the number of API calls made to AWS Bedrock from a user within an hour time period was observed. These events may be part of normal Bedrock operations or may be indicative of enumeration/discovery attempts. Observe the username, role, user agent and source IP to confirm whether this activity is expected. If this signal is triggering frequently, consider excluding certain authorized users via tuning expression.
OUTLIER-S00019.match_expression:
metadata_vendor = "Amazon AWS"
AND metadata_product = "CloudTrail"
AND application = "bedrock.amazonaws.com"
OUTLIER-S00019.name: Outlier in AWS Bedrock API Calls from User
OUTLIER-S00019.name_expression: Outlier in AWS Bedrock API Calls from User: {{user_username}}
OUTLIER-S00019.normalized_description: An outlier in the number of API calls made to AWS Bedrock from a user within an hour time period was observed. These events may be part of normal Bedrock operations or may be indicative of enumeration/discovery attempts. Observe the username, role, user agent and source IP to confirm whether this activity is expected. If this signal is triggering frequently, consider excluding certain authorized users via tuning expression.
OUTLIER-S00019.normalized_signal_name: Outlier in AWS Bedrock API Calls from User: {{user_username}}
OUTLIER-S00019.normalized_summary: Outlier in AWS Bedrock API Calls from User: {{user_username}}
OUTLIER-S00019.summary_expression: Outlier in AWS Bedrock API Calls from User: {{user_username}}

OUTLIER-S00022.name: Outlier in AWS Bedrock Foundation Model Enumeration Calls from User
